I now have a comprehensive picture of both the SOP and the EU AI Act requirements. Let me compile the detailed gap analysis.

---

## Gap Analysis: SOP-CLIN-001 vs. EU AI Act Articles 11 & 17

### Overview

SOP-CLIN-001 is a well-structured validation SOP that addresses many clinical and technical validation elements. However, when measured against the specific requirements of **Article 11** (Technical Documentation, incorporating Annex IV) and **Article 17** (Quality Management System) of the EU AI Act (Regulation (EU) 2024/1689), several material gaps emerge. Below is the detailed analysis.

---

## PART A: GAPS AGAINST ARTICLE 11 (TECHNICAL DOCUMENTATION) & ANNEX IV

Article 11 requires that technical documentation contain, at a minimum, all elements set out in **Annex IV**. The SOP references "Annex IV documentation elements" in Section 5.5 but does not enumerate or operationalize them. The following Annex IV elements are **missing or insufficiently addressed**:

### 1. General Description of the AI System (Annex IV, point 1)

| Requirement | Status in SOP | Gap |
|---|---|---|
| Intended purpose, name of system, version | Partially addressed — Section 5.1 (CEDD) and Section 5.4 (version tagging) | No requirement to document the **provider's name and contact details**, the **date of the system**, or the **instructions for use** as part of the technical documentation package. The SOP focuses on validation artifacts but does not mandate a consolidated system description document. |
| Description of the AI system's interaction with hardware/software | **Missing** | No requirement to document how the CDSS interacts with or integrates into the EHR environment (Meridian ClinicalIQ) in the technical documentation. |
| Versions of relevant software/firmware and any version requirements | Partially addressed — Section 5.4 (code freeze, containerization) | The SOP captures the Docker container SHA-256 digest but does not require documenting all relevant pre-requisite software, firmware versions, or API dependencies. |
| Description of the forms in which the system is placed on the market | **Missing** | No requirement to describe deployment form (e.g., SaaS, on-premise, embedded in EHR). |
| Description of hardware on which the system is intended to run | **Missing** | No documentation requirement for computational/hardware resources needed (cf. Article 13(3)(e)). |
| Where applicable, the instructions for use and/or deployment | **Missing** | The SOP mentions IFU in Policy Statement 3 but does not require the IFU to be part of the technical documentation package. |

### 2. Detailed Description of the System Elements and Development Process (Annex IV, point 2)

| Requirement | Status in SOP | Gap |
|---|---|---|
| Methods and steps performed for development, including where relevant recourse to pre-trained systems | **Missing** | The SOP covers validation only — it does not require documentation of the **development methodology**, training procedures, or whether pre-trained models/third-party components were used. |
| Design specifications (system architecture, algorithms, logic) | **Missing** | No requirement to document system architecture, algorithm design rationale, or decision logic. The SOP references SOP-DS-001 (Model Development and Coding Standard) but does not integrate design specification documentation into the validation/technical documentation workflow. |
| Key design choices and assumptions | **Missing** | No requirement to document why specific model architectures, features, or thresholds were chosen. |
| Description of the system's architecture explaining how software components build on or feed into each other | **Missing** | No architecture documentation requirement. |

### 3. Training Methodologies and Datasets (Annex IV, points 2(c)–2(g))

| Requirement | Status in SOP | Gap |
|---|---|---|
| Training data provenance, scope, and main characteristics | **Missing** | The SOP covers the lockbox/test set but does not require documentation of **training data** sources, characteristics, selection criteria, or provenance. |
| Information on the provenance, scope, and main characteristics of validation and testing datasets | Partially addressed — Section 5.2 (data curation) | The SOP covers the Prospective Validation Set but does not require documenting the **testing dataset** as a distinct concept from the validation set (the EU AI Act distinguishes training, validation, and testing data). |
| Data labelling information (procedures, quality controls) | Partially addressed — Section 5.3 (Gold Standard reader study) | The SOP covers Gold Standard adjudication but does not require documenting the **full labelling methodology** including inter-rater reliability metrics, label quality controls, or any automated labelling used. |
| Data governance measures (Article 10 compliance) | **Missing** | No requirement to document how training/validation/testing datasets comply with Article 10 data governance requirements (e.g., examination for biases, relevance, representativeness, completeness). |

### 4. Risk Management System Documentation (Annex IV, point 3)

| Requirement | Status in SOP | Gap |
|---|---|---|
| Documentation of the risk management system (Article 9) | **Missing** | The SOP references SOP-CLIN-002 (CDSS Risk Management and FMEA Procedure) but does not require the risk management system documentation to be integrated into or cross-referenced from the technical documentation. Article 9 requires a continuous iterative risk management process throughout the entire lifecycle — the SOP's validation focus does not capture this. |

### 5. Human Oversight Measures (Annex IV, point 4 / Article 14)

| Requirement | Status in SOP | Gap |
|---|---|---|
| Description of human oversight measures built into the system | **Missing entirely** | The SOP states the CDSS "must not replace the clinical judgment of a qualified healthcare professional" (Policy Statement 1) but does **not** require documentation of specific human oversight measures as required by Article 14: no mention of human-machine interface tools, override mechanisms, "stop" buttons, automation bias awareness, or the ability to interpret and disregard outputs. |
| Measures to enable deployers to interpret system output | **Missing** | No requirement to document interpretation tools or methods. |

### 6. Performance Metrics and Validation Results (Annex IV, points 5–6)

| Requirement | Status in SOP | Gap |
|---|---|---|
| Performance metrics and validation results | Partially addressed — Section 5.5 (FVR) | The SOP covers sensitivity, specificity, AUC, calibration, and subgroup analysis. However, it does **not** explicitly require documentation of: (a) **robustness testing** (resilience to errors, faults, inconsistencies — Article 15(3)); (b) **cybersecurity testing results** (resilience to adversarial examples, data poisoning — Article 15(4)); (c) **accuracy levels declared** in a format suitable for the instructions for use (Article 15(2)). |
| Testing against reasonably foreseeable misuse | **Missing** | No requirement to test or document system behavior under conditions of reasonably foreseeable misuse. |

### 7. Post-Market Monitoring Plan (Annex IV, point 7 / Article 72)

| Requirement | Status in SOP | Gap |
|---|---|---|
| Post-market monitoring plan as part of technical documentation | Partially addressed — Section 7.1 | The SOP requires a PMCF plan within 30 days of deployment but does **not** require this plan to be integrated into the technical documentation as mandated by Article 72(3) ("The post-market monitoring plan shall be part of the technical documentation referred to in Annex IV"). The plan is treated as a separate post-deployment deliverable. |

### 8. EU Declaration of Conformity & CE Marking (Article 16(g)–(h), Article 47–48)

| Requirement | Status in SOP | Gap |
|---|---|---|
| EU declaration of conformity | **Missing** | No mention of drawing up an EU declaration of conformity. |
| CE marking | **Missing** | No mention of CE marking requirements for high-risk AI systems. |
| Registration in EU database (Article 49) | **Missing** | No mention of the obligation to register stand-alone high-risk AI systems in the EU database. |

### 9. Instructions for Use (Article 13 / Annex IV)

| Requirement | Status in SOP | Gap |
|---|---|---|
| Comprehensive IFU with all Article 13(3) elements | **Missing** | Policy Statement 3 mentions IFU but the SOP does not operationalize the creation, content requirements, or review of IFU as part of the validation/technical documentation workflow. Article 13(3) requires specific elements including accuracy metrics, robustness, cybersecurity, human oversight measures, expected lifetime, maintenance, and computational resources. |

---

## PART B: GAPS AGAINST ARTICLE 17 (QUALITY MANAGEMENT SYSTEM)

Article 17 requires a QMS documented in a "systematic and orderly manner in the form of written policies, procedures and instructions." The SOP references a QMS (Section 10.2: "Our QMS is intended to align with Article 17 requirements") but the SOP itself does not constitute or fully operationalize the QMS elements. Here are the specific gaps:

### 1. Article 17(1)(a): Strategy for Regulatory Compliance

| Requirement | Status in SOP | Gap |
|---|---|---|
| Strategy for regulatory compliance, including conformity assessment procedures | **Missing** | The SOP references "Regulatory Alignment" (Policy Statement 6) but does not include or reference a documented **regulatory compliance strategy**. No mention of which conformity assessment procedure applies (Annex VI vs. Annex VII), no reference to notified body engagement, and no procedure for managing the conformity assessment process. |
| Procedures for management of modifications to the high-risk AI system | **Missing** | The SOP references SOP-CLIN-005 (Clinical Algorithm Change Management and Versioning) but does not integrate modification management into the QMS framework. No procedure for determining when a modification triggers a new conformity assessment. |

### 2. Article 17(1)(b)–(c): Design, Design Control, Design Verification, Quality Control

| Requirement | Status in SOP | Gap |
|---|---|---|
| Techniques, procedures, and systematic actions for design, design control, and design verification | **Missing** | The SOP is a **validation** procedure, not a design control procedure. It does not address design inputs, design outputs, design review, or design verification as systematic actions. |
| Techniques, procedures, and systematic actions for development, quality control, and quality assurance | **Missing** | The SOP references SOP-DS-001 but does not itself establish development QC/QA procedures. |

### 3. Article 17(1)(d): Examination, Test, and Validation Procedures — Before, During, and After Development

| Requirement | Status in SOP | Gap |
|---|---|---|
| Validation procedures to be carried out **before, during, and after** development | Partially addressed | The SOP covers pre-deployment validation (Phase 0–5) and post-deployment surveillance (Section 7). However, it does **not** address validation procedures **during** development (e.g., interim testing, unit testing, integration testing). |
| Frequency with which validation procedures must be carried out | Partially addressed | Section 7.3 specifies monthly, quarterly, and annual cadences for post-market surveillance, but does not specify the frequency of **re-validation** during the development lifecycle or after significant modifications. |

### 4. Article 17(1)(e): Technical Specifications and Standards

| Requirement | Status in SOP | Gap |
|---|---|---|
| Technical specifications, including standards to be applied | Partially addressed — Section 10.2 lists external standards | The SOP lists IEC 62304, ISO 13485, EU AI Act, EU MDR, NIST AI RMF, and HIPAA. However, it does **not** specify: (a) which specific clauses/requirements of these standards apply; (b) what means will be used where harmonized standards are not applied in full or do not cover all requirements. This is a critical Article 17(1)(e) gap. |

### 5. Article 17(1)(f): Systems and Procedures for Data Management

| Requirement | Status in SOP | Gap |
|---|---|---|
| Data acquisition, collection, analysis, labelling, storage, filtration, mining, aggregation, retention | Partially addressed — Section 5.2 (data curation) | The SOP covers de-identification, hold-out set creation, and lockbox procedures. However, it does **not** address: **data acquisition** governance (how data is sourced from M-DL), **data labelling** as a systematic QMS procedure (beyond the reader study), **data retention** policies, **data filtration/mining/aggregation** procedures, or **data provenance** documentation for training data. |

### 6. Article 17(1)(g): The Risk Management System (Article 9)

| Requirement | Status in SOP | Gap |
|---|---|---|
| Risk management system established, implemented, documented, and maintained | **Missing from SOP scope** | The SOP references SOP-CLIN-002 (FMEA Procedure) but does not itself establish or integrate the Article 9 risk management system. Article 9 requires: (a) identification and analysis of known and reasonably foreseeable risks to health, safety, or fundamental rights; (b) estimation and evaluation of risks under intended use and reasonably foreseeable misuse; (c) evaluation of risks from post-market monitoring data; (d) adoption of appropriate and targeted risk management measures. The SOP's FMEA reference is insufficient — the QMS must **contain** the risk management system, not merely reference it. |

### 7. Article 17(1)(h): Post-Market Monitoring System (Article 72)

| Requirement | Status in SOP | Gap |
|---|---|---|
| Setting-up, implementation, and maintenance of a post-market monitoring system | Partially addressed — Section 7 | The SOP has strong post-market monitoring KPIs (PSI, override rate, AEs, bias/fairness). However, Article 72 requires the system to "actively and systematically collect, document and analyse relevant data... on the performance of high-risk AI systems throughout their lifetime" and to "allow the provider to evaluate the continuous compliance." The SOP's monitoring is KPI-driven but does not explicitly link back to **continuous compliance evaluation** against all Chapter III, Section 2 requirements. |

### 8. Article 17(1)(i): Procedures for Reporting of Serious Incidents (Article 73)

| Requirement | Status in SOP | Gap |
|---|---|---|
| Procedures related to reporting of serious incidents | **Critically missing** | The SOP's escalation procedure (Section 8.2) covers internal triage, emergency CAB, and RCA. However, it does **not** include procedures for **reporting serious incidents to market surveillance authorities** as required by Article 73. Specifically missing: (a) the **15-day reporting deadline** (or 2-day for widespread infringement, or 10-day for death); (b) identification of which **Member State market surveillance authorities** to notify; (c) the content requirements of a serious incident report; (d) the obligation to cooperate with competent authorities during investigations. The SOP's breach notification language (Section 8.2, step 4) is vague — "prompt notification to the relevant authorities in accordance with applicable timelines" — and does not operationalize Article 73. |

### 9. Article 17(1)(j): Handling of Communication with Competent Authorities

| Requirement | Status in SOP | Gap |
|---|---|---|
| Handling of communication with national competent authorities, notified bodies, other operators, customers, or other interested parties | **Missing** | No procedure for communicating with national competent authorities, notified bodies, or other external stakeholders. The Regulatory Affairs Specialist role (Section 3) is "Consulted" but no communication protocol is defined. |

### 10. Article 17(1)(k): Systems and Procedures for Record-Keeping

| Requirement | Status in SOP | Gap |
|---|---|---|
| Systems and procedures for record-keeping of all relevant documentation and information | Partially addressed | The SOP references MDMS for artifact storage (Section 5.6) and audit logs (Section 6.1). However, it does not establish a **systematic record-keeping procedure** covering: retention periods, document control, versioning of technical documentation, or ensuring records are kept up-to-date throughout the system lifecycle. |

### 11. Article 17(1)(l): Resource Management

| Requirement | Status in SOP | Gap |
|---|---|---|
| Resource management, including security-of-supply related measures | **Missing** | No mention of resource management, supply chain security, or continuity of critical resources (e.g., compute infrastructure, third-party model dependencies, CRO availability). |

### 12. Article 17(1)(m): Accountability Framework

| Requirement | Status in SOP | Gap |
|---|---|---|
| Accountability framework setting out responsibilities of management and other staff with regard to all Article 17 aspects | Partially addressed — Section 3 (RACI matrix) | The RACI matrix covers validation-specific roles but does **not** assign accountability for: (a) regulatory compliance strategy; (b) QMS maintenance; (c) conformity assessment; (d) serious incident reporting to authorities; (e) communication with notified bodies; (f) post-market monitoring system maintenance; (g) record-keeping. The accountability framework must cover **all** Article 17(1) aspects, not just validation. |

---

## SUMMARY OF CRITICAL MISSING ELEMENTS

### High-Severity Gaps (likely to prevent conformity):

1. **No serious incident reporting procedure to market surveillance authorities** (Article 17(1)(i) / Article 73)
2. **No human oversight measures documented** (Article 14 / Annex IV point 4)
3. **No regulatory compliance strategy or conformity assessment procedure** (Article 17(1)(a))
4. **No EU declaration of conformity or CE marking procedure** (Article 16(g)–(h), Articles 47–48)
5. **No integration of the risk management system into the QMS** (Article 17(1)(g) / Article 9)
6. **No design control or design verification procedures** (Article 17(1)(b))
7. **No communication protocol with competent authorities and notified bodies** (Article 17(1)(j))

### Medium-Severity Gaps (significant documentation deficiencies):

8. **Training data documentation missing** (Annex IV point 2)
9. **System architecture and design specification documentation missing** (Annex IV point 2)
10. **No robustness or cybersecurity testing documentation** (Article 15 / Annex IV)
11. **Post-market monitoring plan not integrated into technical documentation** (Article 72(3))
12. **No systematic record-keeping procedure** (Article 17(1)(k))
13. **No resource management or security-of-supply provisions** (Article 17(1)(l))
14. **Instructions for Use not operationalized** (Article 13)
15. **No testing under reasonably foreseeable misuse** (Article 9(2)(b))

### Lower-Severity Gaps (refinements needed):

16. **Accountability framework does not cover all Article 17 aspects** (Article 17(1)(m))
17. **Data management procedures incomplete** (Article 17(1)(f)) — missing data acquisition governance, labelling procedures, retention policies
18. **Validation procedures during development not addressed** (Article 17(1)(d))
19. **Technical specifications gap analysis not performed** (Article 17(1)(e))
20. **No explicit distinction between validation and testing datasets** per EU AI Act definitions

---

**Compliance level: PARTIAL** — The SOP demonstrates strong clinical validation rigor and awareness of the EU AI Act (Policy Statement 6, Section 10.2), but it remains fundamentally a **validation SOP**, not a **QMS framework** or a **technical documentation control procedure**. To achieve conformity with Articles 11 and 17, Meridian would need either a substantial revision of SOP-CLIN-001 to incorporate the missing QMS and technical documentation elements, or (more appropriately) a suite of complementary SOPs that together form the QMS, with SOP-CLIN-001 serving as the validation component within that broader framework.

scores.judge_rationale: The candidate answer thoroughly identifies all major missing elements from the SOP against Articles 11 and 17 and Annex IV, matching the reference's scope, but it cites the EU AI Act generally without pinpointing specific sections like Annex IV points or Article 17 sub-elements in a structured traceability format.

question.question: Reviewing SOP-LEGC-005 (Regulatory Filing and Reporting) against GDPR's transparency and transfer obligations, what specific elements are MISSING?
question.expected_answer: Missing: (1) Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs) or alternative Chapter V transfer mechanisms for cross-border filings — international transfers are acknowledged but the operational mechanism is not specified; (2) transfer-impact-assessment procedures considering the legal environment of recipient jurisdictions; (3) Article 13/14 specificity in privacy notices triggered by filing-related processing — purposes, lawful basis, recipients (regulators), retention, data-subject rights; (4) supplementary measures (encryption, pseudonymisation) tied to high-risk filings; (5) records of processing (Article 30) specifically for filing-related processing activities.

output.answer:
I now have a comprehensive picture of both the SOP and the relevant GDPR provisions. Let me compile my analysis.

---

## Gap Analysis: SOP-LEGC-005 vs. GDPR Transparency & Transfer Obligations

### Summary

SOP-LEGC-005 (Regulatory Filing and Reporting) contains only a single, high-level policy statement addressing GDPR (Policy Statement #6), and it is materially deficient against the GDPR's transparency (Articles 5, 12–14, 34) and international transfer (Articles 44–49) obligations. Below is a detailed, element-by-element identification of what is **MISSING**.

---

### A. TRANSPARENCY OBLIGATIONS — MISSING ELEMENTS

#### 1. No Article 13 / Article 14 Notice Integration into the Filing Workflow

**What GDPR requires:** Articles 13 and 14 mandate that when personal data is collected (Art. 13) or obtained from a third party (Art. 14), the controller must provide the data subject with specific information: identity/contact details of the controller and DPO, purposes and legal basis of processing, recipients/categories of recipients, transfer information (adequacy decision or safeguards), retention periods, and the existence of data subject rights (access, rectification, erasure, restriction, portability, objection, complaint to a supervisory authority).

**What SOP-LEGC-005 says:** Policy Statement #6 states only: *"Data subjects are informed about processing activities through Meridian's Privacy Notices posted on the corporate website."*

**What is MISSING:**
- No procedure requiring the RFO or Regulatory Operations Analyst to verify that the specific processing of personal data *within a given regulatory filing* is covered by an existing privacy notice — or to trigger a notice update if it is not.
- No requirement to provide a layered or "just-in-time" notice to data subjects whose personal data is included in a regulatory submission, particularly where the filing involves data not originally collected for that regulatory purpose (triggering Art. 14).
- No mechanism to determine whether the filing involves data collected directly (Art. 13) or indirectly (Art. 14), which changes the notice content required.
- No mention of the obligation under Art. 13(1)(e) / Art. 14(1)(e) to disclose **recipients or categories of recipients** — including the specific regulatory addressee (e.g., FDA, EU supervisory authority, state AG).
- No mention of the obligation to inform data subjects of the **legal basis** for processing (Art. 6(1)) their data in the filing context.

#### 2. No Article 12 Transparency Standard for Filing Communications

**What GDPR requires:** Article 12 requires that all information to data subjects be provided in a *"concise, transparent, intelligible and easily accessible form, using clear and plain language."*

**What is MISSING:**
- The SOP contains no requirement that privacy notices or data-subject communications related to regulatory filings meet the Art. 12 "clear and plain language" standard.
- No review gate in the G1–G5 workflow checks whether privacy disclosures associated with a filing are Art. 12-compliant.
- No mention of the Art. 12(7) option to use standardized icons for transparency.

#### 3. No Article 34 Breach Communication Procedure for Filing-Related Breaches

**What GDPR requires:** Article 34 mandates that when a personal data breach is likely to result in *high risk* to data subjects, the controller must communicate the breach to affected data subjects *"without undue delay"* in clear and plain language, describing the nature of the breach and the measures taken.

**What SOP-LEGC-005 says:** The SOP references GDPR breach notifications in Section 5.2(2): *"For GDPR breach notifications, the template is in the ServiceNow Security Incident Response module."* But this is limited to the *supervisory authority* notification (Art. 33).

**What is MISSING:**
- No procedure for determining when a regulatory filing-related breach triggers the Art. 34 obligation to notify **data subjects** (as distinct from the Art. 33 obligation to notify the supervisory authority).
- No template, workflow, or RACI assignment for Art. 34 data subject communications.
- No integration with the AIMS (C-07) or Security Incident Response Lead role for the data-subject-facing communication obligation.

#### 4. No Data Subject Rights Facilitation in the Filing Context

**What GDPR requires:** Article 12(2) requires the controller to *"facilitate the exercise of data subject rights"* under Articles 15–22. Article 15 gives data subjects the right to know, among other things, the recipients in third countries and the Art. 46 safeguards.

**What is MISSING:**
- No procedure for handling a data subject access request (DSAR) that specifically asks about personal data included in a regulatory filing.
- No mechanism to ensure that when a filing is submitted containing personal data, the ROPA (Art. 30) is updated to reflect the regulatory addressee as a recipient — which is essential for responding to Art. 15 requests.
- No mention of the Art. 19 obligation to notify recipients (including regulators) of any subsequent rectification or erasure of personal data contained in a filed submission.

---

### B. INTERNATIONAL TRANSFER OBLIGATIONS — MISSING ELEMENTS

#### 5. No Transfer Mechanism Identification or Documentation

**What GDPR requires:** Articles 44–49 establish a tiered framework: transfers are permitted on the basis of an adequacy decision (Art. 45), appropriate safeguards (Art. 46 — SCCs, BCRs, codes of conduct, certification), or derogations (Art. 49). Article 13(1)(f) and Article 14(1)(f) require the controller to inform data subjects of the specific transfer mechanism relied upon and *"the means by which to obtain a copy of them or where they have been made available."*

**What SOP-LEGC-005 says:** Policy Statement #6 states only: *"When personal data is transferred from the EU to Meridian's US-based analytics teams for a regulatory submission, Meridian ensures appropriate safeguards are in place."*

**What is MISSING:**
- **No identification of the specific transfer mechanism.** The SOP never names whether Meridian relies on the EU-US Data Privacy Framework (DPF), Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs), Binding Corporate Rules (BCRs), or an Art. 49 derogation. "Appropriate safeguards" is a conclusory label, not a documented mechanism.
- **No Transfer Impact Assessment (TIA) requirement.** Following *Schrems II* and EDPB Recommendations 01/2020, controllers must conduct a TIA assessing whether the law of the third country ensures a level of protection essentially equivalent to EU law, and whether supplementary measures are needed. The SOP is silent on this.
- **No procedure for documenting the transfer mechanism per filing.** The SOP does not require the Evidence Package (Section 5.2(3)) to include the relevant SCCs, DPF certification, or BCR documentation for the specific transfer.
- **No onward transfer controls.** Article 44(1) requires that onward transfers from the third country to another third country also comply with Chapter V. The SOP does not address what happens when a US-based team further shares EU personal data with Meridian's Singapore or Canada operations (both in scope per Section 1.2).

#### 6. No Article 30 ROPA Integration for Filing-Related Transfers

**What GDPR requires:** Article 30 requires the ROPA to include *"transfers of personal data to a third country or an international organisation, including the identification of that third country or international organisation and... the documentation of suitable safeguards."*

**What SOP-LEGC-005 says:** Section 3.1 mentions the DPO is RFO for *"Records of Processing Activities (ROPA) summaries when requested by an SA."*

**What is MISSING:**
- No procedure requiring that each regulatory filing involving EU personal data transferred to a third country triggers a ROPA update identifying: the recipient regulator, the third country, and the specific Art. 44–49 transfer basis.
- The SOP treats ROPA as a reactive document (provided "when requested by an SA") rather than a living, proactively maintained record as Art. 30 requires.

#### 7. No Article 35 DPIA Trigger for High-Risk Filing Transfers

**What GDPR requires:** Article 35 mandates a Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA) where processing — *"in particular using new technologies"* — is likely to result in high risk to data subjects. Systematic transfer of sensitive health data (which Meridian processes as a health technology company) to foreign regulators likely triggers this.

**What is MISSING:**
- No DPIA trigger or checklist in the filing workflow to assess whether a particular regulatory submission involving EU personal data requires a DPIA before submission.
- No mention of the Art. 36 prior consultation obligation with the supervisory authority if the DPIA indicates unmitigated high risk.

#### 8. No Article 28 Processor Due Diligence for Filing Vendors

**What GDPR requires:** Article 28 requires that processors provide *"sufficient guarantees"* and that processing be governed by a contract containing specific mandatory terms, including restrictions on international transfers.

**What is MISSING:**
- The SOP's scope includes *"third-party vendors who have a role in the creation, review, approval, or submission of a regulatory filing"* (Section 1.2), but there is no procedure ensuring that vendors handling EU personal data in the filing process are bound by Art. 28-compliant data processing agreements.
- No requirement that vendor-assisted transfers to third countries be covered by the same Art. 46 safeguards.

---

### C. STRUCTURAL / GOVERNANCE GAPS

#### 9. No GDPR-Specific Gate in the Review Workflow

The G1–G5 workflow (Section 5.3) contains no gate dedicated to GDPR compliance review. G3 (Legal/Compliance Review) is the closest, but it is framed around *"legal privilege, interpretation of regulation, consistency with past filings, and overall defensibility"* — not a structured GDPR checklist covering transparency, transfer, data minimization, or purpose limitation.

#### 10. No Data Minimization or Purpose Limitation Check

Article 5(1)(b) and (c) require that personal data be *"adequate, relevant and limited to what is necessary"* (data minimization) and not further processed in a manner incompatible with the original purpose (purpose limitation). The SOP contains no procedure requiring the RFO to assess whether the personal data included in a regulatory filing is the *minimum necessary* to satisfy the regulatory obligation, or whether the filing constitutes a compatible purpose.

#### 11. No Retention / Storage Limitation for Personal Data in Filings

Article 5(1)(e) requires that personal data be kept *"for no longer than is necessary."* Section 5.5 addresses archival of the filing package but does not distinguish between the retention of the filing for regulatory purposes and the retention of the *personal data within it*. There is no procedure for redacting or deleting personal data from archived filings once the retention purpose is fulfilled.

---

### Compliance Level: **GAP**

The SOP contains a single aspirational policy statement (Policy #6) that gestures toward GDPR compliance but lacks the procedural specificity, workflow integration, documentation requirements, and governance mechanisms needed to satisfy GDPR's transparency (Articles 5, 12–14, 34) and international transfer (Articles 44–49) obligations. The deficiencies span notice requirements, transfer mechanism documentation, TIA/DPIA integration, ROPA maintenance, data subject rights facilitation, processor controls, and data minimization — each of which is independently material.

scores.judge_rationale: The candidate answer comprehensively identifies all missing elements from the reference (SCCs/transfer mechanisms, TIA, Art. 13/14 notice specifics, supplementary measures, Art. 30 ROPA) and correctly cites GDPR Articles 13, 14, 30, and Chapter V.

question.question: Reviewing SOP-ITOP-005 (Capacity Planning and Management) against SOC 2 Common Criteria CC7 (monitoring) and CC8 (change management), what specific controls are MISSING?
question.expected_answer: Missing: (1) scheduled tabletop exercises for incident-response scenarios (CC7.4) — the plan exists but periodic testing is absent; (2) defined alert thresholds and escalation paths for capacity-related anomalies (CC7.2/7.3); (3) explicit segregation of duties between change requester, approver, and implementer for capacity changes (CC8.1); (4) post-incident review procedures feeding lessons learned back to monitoring and change processes (CC7.5).

## Gap Analysis: SOP-ITOP-005 vs. SOC 2 CC7 (System Operations) and CC8 (Change Management)

### Summary

SOP-ITOP-005 is a mature, well-structured document with strong controls around capacity monitoring, threshold-based alerting, and IaC-driven change execution with segregation of duties. However, when mapped against the full text of SOC 2 CC7 and CC8 criteria (and the related CC4 monitoring-of-controls criteria), several specific controls are **missing or insufficiently addressed**.

---

## MISSING CONTROLS — CC7 (System Operations / Monitoring)

### 1. CC7.1 — Vulnerability Scanning (GAP)

**What CC7.1 requires:** "The entity conducts infrastructure and software vulnerability scans designed to identify potential vulnerabilities or misconfigurations on a periodic basis and after significant changes are made to the environment. Action is taken to remediate identified deficiencies in a timely manner."

**What the SOP provides:** The SOP is silent on vulnerability scanning. Section 5.1 defines performance baselines (CPU, memory, latency, etc.) and Section 5.2 defines capacity threshold monitoring, but there is no provision for vulnerability scanning of the infrastructure components being managed. The SOP mentions Security Hub auto-remediation for manual AWS Console changes (Section 6.1), but this is a detective control for unauthorized access, not a scheduled vulnerability scanning program.

**Missing:** No requirement to conduct periodic vulnerability scans of in-scope infrastructure, nor to scan after significant capacity changes (e.g., after vertical scaling of a production database or adding new EKS nodes). No documented remediation timeline for vulnerabilities discovered on capacity-managed resources.

---

### 2. CC7.1 — Change-Detection Mechanisms / File Integrity Monitoring (GAP)

**What CC7.1 requires:** "The IT system includes a change-detection mechanism (for example, file integrity monitoring tools) to alert personnel to unauthorized modifications of critical system files, configuration files, or content files."

**What the SOP provides:** The SOP relies on Git-based IaC with branch protection and CODEOWNERS (Section 6.2), which prevents unauthorized changes through the approved pipeline. However, there is no file integrity monitoring (FIM) or change-detection mechanism at the OS or configuration level to detect changes made outside the IaC pipeline (e.g., via "break glass" console access or a compromised credential).

**Missing:** No file integrity monitoring or configuration change-detection tooling deployed on production infrastructure to detect and alert on unauthorized modifications to critical system files, configuration files, or content files outside the approved CI/CD pipeline.

---

### 3. CC7.1 — Detection of Unknown or Unauthorized Components (GAP)

**What CC7.1 requires:** "Procedures are in place to detect the introduction of unknown or unauthorized components."

**What the SOP provides:** The SOP does not address detection of unauthorized infrastructure components (e.g., rogue EC2 instances, unauthorized EKS pods, unapproved SaaS connections). The cost safeguard in Section 6.3 terminates sandbox instances on budget overrun, but this is a cost control, not a security detection mechanism.

**Missing:** No procedure to detect and alert on the introduction of unknown or unauthorized infrastructure components in production environments.

---

### 4. CC7.2 — Anomaly Detection for Malicious Acts (PARTIAL GAP)

**What CC7.2 requires:** "The entity monitors system components and the operation of those components for anomalies that are indicative of malicious acts, natural disasters, and errors... Implements Detection Policies, Procedures, and Tools... to identify potential intrusions, inappropriate access, and anomalies in the operation of or unusual activity on systems."

**What the SOP provides:** The SOP's monitoring (Section 5.2) is exclusively performance/capacity-oriented — CPU, memory, disk I/O, network throughput, latency, error rate, Kafka lag. These detect capacity exhaustion and performance degradation but are not designed to detect malicious activity (e.g., data exfiltration patterns, unusual API call patterns, privilege escalation, lateral movement).

**Missing:** No integration of security-oriented anomaly detection (e.g., unusual network traffic patterns, unexpected outbound connections, anomalous API call rates to sensitive endpoints) into the capacity monitoring framework. The SOP treats monitoring as purely a performance discipline.

---

### 5. CC7.2 — Monitoring Detection Tools for Effective Operation (GAP)

**What CC7.2 requires:** "Management has implemented processes to monitor and maintain the effectiveness of detection tools."

**What the SOP provides:** The SOP defines monitoring of infrastructure but does not include a process to monitor whether the monitoring tools themselves (Datadog agents, synthetic checks, dead man's switches) are functioning correctly. There is no health-check procedure for the monitoring stack.

**Missing:** No procedure to verify that Datadog agents are running, synthetic checks are executing, and alerting pipelines are operational across all in-scope environments.

---

### 6. CC7.3 — Security Event Evaluation and Analysis Procedures (GAP)

**What CC7.3 requires:** "The entity evaluates security events to determine whether they could or have resulted in a failure... Develops and Implements Procedures to Analyze Security Incidents — Procedures are in place to analyze security incidents and determine system impact."

**What the SOP provides:** Section 4.5 references SOP-ISEC-002 for incident response and Section 5.5 defines Post-Incident Review (PIR) for capacity incidents. However, the SOP does not define how capacity-related anomalies should be evaluated to determine whether they represent security events (e.g., distinguishing a DDoS-driven capacity spike from a legitimate traffic surge).

**Missing:** No documented procedure for evaluating capacity anomalies through a security lens to determine whether they constitute security events requiring different escalation and response paths.

---

### 7. CC4.1 / CC4.2 — Ongoing Monitoring of Controls Themselves (GAP)

**What CC4.1 requires:** "The entity selects, develops, and performs ongoing and/or separate evaluations to ascertain whether the components of internal control are present and functioning." CC4.2 adds: "The entity evaluates and communicates internal control deficiencies in a timely manner."

**What the SOP provides:** The SOP defines KPIs (Section 7.1) and reporting cadences (Section 7.2), but there is no process for periodically testing whether the capacity management controls themselves are operating effectively — e.g., whether alerts actually fire when thresholds are breached, whether the PR approval gating actually works, or whether the segregation of duties in ServiceNow is functioning as designed.

**Missing:** No periodic control testing program (separate evaluations) to validate that the capacity management controls described in the SOP are present and functioning. No process for communicating control deficiencies to senior management.

---

## MISSING CONTROLS — CC8 (Change Management)

### 8. CC8.1 — Testing System Changes Prior to Implementation (GAP)

**What CC8.1 requires:** "Tests System Changes — A process is in place to test internally developed and acquired system changes prior to implementation into the production environment. Examples of testing may include unit, integration, regression, static and dynamic application source code, quality assurance, or automated testing."

**What the SOP provides:** The scaling runbooks (Sections 5.4.1 and 5.4.2) describe the IaC change process: create branch → modify Terraform/Helm → open PR → `terraform plan` / `helm diff` → approve → merge → apply. The `terraform plan` and `helm diff` outputs show what *will* change but are not tests — they are dry-run diffs. There is no requirement to test the capacity change in a pre-production/staging environment before applying to production.

**Missing:** No pre-production testing requirement for capacity changes. The SOP does not mandate that scaling changes be validated in staging/UAT environments before production deployment, despite the existence of pre-production environments listed in the scope (Section 1.2).

---

### 9. CC8.1 — Emergency Change Procedure (GAP)

**What CC8.1 requires:** "Provides for Changes Necessary in Emergency Situations — A process is in place for authorizing, designing, testing, approving, and implementing changes necessary in emergency situations (that is, changes that need to be implemented in an urgent time frame)."

**What the SOP provides:** Section 8.1 mentions emergency security patches as an example exception scenario, and Section 8.3 allows the CTO to "override standard change management lead times for critical fixes." However, the SOP does not define a formal, documented emergency change procedure with its own control path (e.g., post-implementation review, retrospective documentation requirements, mandatory post-emergency testing).

**Missing:** No formal emergency change procedure. The exception process (Section 8.2) is designed for policy exceptions, not for operational emergency changes. There is no defined "break-glass" change path that preserves auditability while allowing urgent capacity scaling during a Tier 3 emergency.

---

### 10. CC8.1 — Patch Management (GAP)

**What CC8.1 requires:** "Manages Patch Changes — A process is in place to identify, evaluate, test, approve, and implement patches in a timely manner on infrastructure and software."

**What the SOP provides:** The SOP is entirely silent on patch management. Capacity changes (scaling) are covered, but the routine patching of operating systems, container images, database engines, and other infrastructure components — which directly affects capacity and availability — is not addressed.

**Missing:** No integration of patch management into the capacity planning lifecycle. Patches may require maintenance windows, capacity headroom during rolling updates, or temporary resource increases — none of which are addressed.

---

### 11. CC8.1 — Baseline Configuration of IT Technology (GAP)

**What CC8.1 requires:** "Creates Baseline Configuration of IT Technology — A baseline configuration of IT and control systems is created and maintained."

**What the SOP provides:** Section 5.1 defines *performance* baselines (p95 of CPU, memory, etc.) but does not define or maintain *configuration* baselines (e.g., hardened AMI versions, approved instance types, standard security group configurations, EKS node image versions).

**Missing:** No configuration baseline is defined or maintained for the infrastructure components governed by this SOP. Performance baselines exist, but configuration baselines (which CC8.1 explicitly requires) do not.

---

### 12. CC8.1 — Identifies and Evaluates System Changes (PARTIAL GAP)

**What CC8.1 requires:** "Identifies and Evaluates System Changes — Objectives affected by system changes are identified, and the ability of the modified system to support the achievement of the objectives is evaluated throughout the system development life cycle."

**What the SOP provides:** The Change Request process (Section 4.4) requires identification of "capacity impact," and the vertical scaling runbook (Section 5.4.2) requires a rollback plan. However, there is no requirement to evaluate how a capacity change affects other system objectives — e.g., security posture (does a new instance type change the attack surface?), compliance (does adding a new region change data residency?), or cost (beyond the budget alert).

**Missing:** No holistic impact assessment requirement for capacity changes that evaluates effects on security, compliance, data residency, or other system objectives beyond capacity and cost.

---

### 13. CC8.1 — Identifies Changes Required to Remediate Incidents (PARTIAL GAP)

**What CC8.1 requires:** "Identifies Changes in Infrastructure, Data, Software, and Procedures Required to Remediate Incidents — Changes in infrastructure, data, software, and procedures required to remediate incidents are identified and the change process is initiated upon identification."

**What the SOP provides:** Section 5.5 (Post-Incident Review) creates Jira action items for gaps. Section 4.5 references SOP-ISEC-002. However, the SOP does not explicitly require that infrastructure changes identified during incident remediation be routed through the formal change management process (ServiceNow CHG). The PIR creates Jira tickets, not Change Requests.

**Missing:** No explicit requirement that capacity-related changes identified during incident post-mortems be initiated through the formal ServiceNow Change Management process.

---

## Summary Table of Missing Controls

| # | SOC 2 Criterion | Point of Focus | Gap Severity | Missing Control |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | CC7.1 | Conducts Vulnerability Scans | **High** | Periodic vulnerability scanning of capacity-managed infrastructure; post-change scanning |
| 2 | CC7.1 | Implements Change-Detection Mechanisms | **High** | File integrity monitoring / configuration change detection outside IaC pipeline |
| 3 | CC7.1 | Detects Unknown or Unauthorized Components | **Medium** | Detection of rogue/unauthorized infrastructure components |
| 4 | CC7.2 | Implements Detection Policies for Malicious Acts | **High** | Security-oriented anomaly detection (not just performance) |
| 5 | CC7.2 | Monitors Detection Tools for Effective Operation | **Medium** | Health monitoring of the monitoring stack itself |
| 6 | CC7.3 | Develops Procedures to Analyze Security Incidents | **Medium** | Procedure to distinguish security events from pure capacity events |
| 7 | CC4.1/4.2 | Ongoing/Separate Evaluations of Controls | **High** | Periodic control testing; communication of control deficiencies |
| 8 | CC8.1 | Tests System Changes | **High** | Pre-production testing of capacity changes in staging/UAT |
| 9 | CC8.1 | Provides for Changes in Emergency Situations | **High** | Formal emergency/"break-glass" change procedure |
| 10 | CC8.1 | Manages Patch Changes | **Medium** | Patch management integration into capacity lifecycle |
| 11 | CC8.1 | Creates Baseline Configuration of IT Technology | **Medium** | Configuration baselines (not just performance baselines) |
| 12 | CC8.1 | Identifies and Evaluates System Changes | **Medium** | Holistic impact assessment beyond capacity/cost |
| 13 | CC8.1 | Identifies Changes to Remediate Incidents | **Low** | Explicit routing of PIR-identified changes through formal CHG process |

---

## Additional Observation: Tabletop Exercises

Section 4.5 of the SOP itself acknowledges a gap: *"Although an incident response plan has been documented to address capacity-driven outages, regular tabletop exercises to rehearse response procedures are not currently scheduled."* This is relevant to **CC7.4** (incident response program execution) and **CC4.1** (ongoing evaluations). The SOP candidly admits this deficiency but does not assign an owner or target date to remediate it.

---

**Compliance level: Partial** — SOP-ITOP-005 has strong operational capacity management controls but is missing critical security-monitoring integration (CC7) and several change management safeguards (CC8) that a SOC 2 auditor would expect to see in a suitably designed control environment.

scores.judge_rationale: The candidate identifies the missing tabletop exercises and post-incident review gaps but omits the required alert thresholds/escalation paths and segregation of duties for capacity changes, and cites CC7.4/CC4.1 instead of the expected CC7.2-CC7.5 and CC8.1.

question.question: Reviewing SOP-LEGC-005 (Regulatory Filing and Reporting) against HIPAA's breach-reporting and de-identification requirements, what specific elements are MISSING?
question.expected_answer: Missing: (1) de-identification methodology for PHI appearing in filings — neither Safe Harbor (§164.514(b)(2)) nor Expert Determination (§164.514(b)(1)) is specified; (2) breach-notification procedures with timelines (§164.404 60-day individual notice, §164.408 HHS reporting), required notice content, and the low-probability-of-compromise risk assessment; (3) minimum-necessary policy (§164.502(b)) for PHI accessible to filing personnel; (4) accounting-of-disclosures procedures (§164.528) for filing-related disclosures.

## Gap Analysis: SOP-LEGC-005 vs. HIPAA Breach-Reporting & De-Identification Requirements

After a line-by-line review of SOP-LEGC-005 against the HIPAA Breach Notification Rule (45 CFR §§ 164.400–164.414) and the De-Identification Standard (§ 164.514), I have identified the following **specific missing elements**:

---

### A. BREACH REPORTING GAPS

#### 1. No Breach Risk Assessment Procedure (Missing: § 164.402(2))

The SOP is entirely silent on the **four-factor risk assessment** that HIPAA mandates to determine whether an impermissible acquisition, access, use, or disclosure of PHI constitutes a reportable breach. Under § 164.402(2), an incident is *presumed* to be a breach unless the covered entity demonstrates a "low probability that the PHI has been compromised" based on a risk assessment of at least:

| Factor | Required by § 164.402(2) | Present in SOP-LEGC-005? |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Nature and extent of PHI involved, including types of identifiers and likelihood of re-identification | Mandatory | **MISSING** |
| (ii) The unauthorized person who used the PHI or to whom disclosure was made | Mandatory | **MISSING** |
| (iii) Whether the PHI was actually acquired or viewed | Mandatory | **MISSING** |
| (iv) The extent to which risk to the PHI has been mitigated | Mandatory | **MISSING** |

The SOP references "security incident" reporting (via the Security Incident Response Lead / Deputy CISO) and the AIMS system for AI-related incidents, but neither incorporates the HIPAA-specific breach risk assessment framework. The SOP does not define who performs this assessment, how it is documented, or how the burden-of-proof demonstration is preserved.

---

#### 2. No Individual Notification Procedure (Missing: § 164.404)

The SOP contains **zero provisions** for notifying affected individuals following a breach of unsecured PHI. HIPAA § 164.404 requires:

- **Timing:** "Without unreasonable delay and in no case later than 60 calendar days after discovery" (§ 164.404(b))
- **Content:** Five specific elements (§ 164.404(c)(1)(A)–(E)):
  - (A) Description of what happened, including breach date and discovery date
  - (B) Types of unsecured PHI involved
  - (C) Steps individuals should take to protect themselves
  - (D) What the covered entity is doing to investigate, mitigate, and prevent further breaches
  - (E) Contact procedures (toll-free number, email, website, or postal address)
- **Plain language requirement** (§ 164.404(c)(2))
- **Method:** First-class mail (or email if the individual agrees) (§ 164.404(d)), with substitute notice procedures if contact information is insufficient or out-of-date (§ 164.404(d)(2))

**None of these elements appear in SOP-LEGC-005.**

---

#### 3. No Media Notification Procedure (Missing: § 164.406)

For breaches involving **more than 500 residents of a State or jurisdiction**, HIPAA § 164.406 requires notification to "prominent media outlets serving the State or jurisdiction." The SOP has no mention of media notification thresholds, procedures, or responsibility assignments.

---

#### 4. No HHS Secretary Notification Procedure (Missing: § 164.408)

HIPAA § 164.408 requires:

- **500+ individuals:** Notification to the Secretary "contemporaneously" with individual notice, via the HHS web portal (§ 164.408(b))
- **<500 individuals:** Maintain a log and submit annually within 60 days of calendar year-end (§ 164.408(c))

The SOP's Filing Calendar and submission procedures (Section 5) are oriented toward periodic regulatory filings (SR 11-7, EU MDR, GDPR), not event-driven HHS breach notifications. There is no mention of the HHS Breach Portal, no template for HHS notification, and no assignment of responsibility for this specific submission.

---

#### 5. No Business Associate Notification Workflow (Missing: § 164.410)

HIPAA § 164.410 requires that a business associate notify the covered entity of a breach "without unreasonable delay and in no case later than 60 calendar days after discovery." The SOP:

- Defines "BA" in the glossary (Section 2) but never operationalizes BA breach notification
- Does not specify how Meridian (as a covered entity) receives, triages, and acts upon BA breach notifications
- Does not address the BA's obligation to identify each affected individual "to the extent possible" (§ 164.410(c)(1))
- Does not address how Meridian (as a BA to other covered entities) would discharge its own notification obligations

---

#### 6. No Definition of "Unsecured PHI" or Encryption Safe Harbor (Missing: § 164.402, HITECH § 13402(h))

The SOP never defines "unsecured protected health information" — a threshold concept for the entire breach notification framework. HIPAA and HITECH provide that PHI rendered "unusable, unreadable, or indecipherable" through encryption or destruction per HHS guidance is not "unsecured" and thus does not trigger notification. The SOP is silent on this critical distinction.

---

#### 7. No Breach Log / Documentation Requirement for Sub-500 Breaches (Missing: § 164.408(c))

HIPAA requires covered entities to maintain a log of breaches involving fewer than 500 individuals and submit them annually to HHS. The SOP has no provision for maintaining such a log, nor does it assign responsibility for the annual submission.

---

#### 8. No Law Enforcement Delay Provision (Missing: § 164.412)

HIPAA § 164.412 permits delayed notification if a law enforcement official states that notification would impede a criminal investigation. The SOP's exception handling (Section 8) does not account for this scenario.

---

### B. DE-IDENTIFICATION GAPS

#### 9. No De-Identification Standard or Procedure (Missing: § 164.514(a)–(b))

The SOP is completely silent on de-identification. HIPAA § 164.514 provides two alternative paths:

- **Expert Determination** (§ 164.514(b)(1)): A qualified person applies statistical/scientific principles, determines the risk of re-identification is "very small," and documents the methods and results.
- **Safe Harbor** (§ 164.514(b)(2)): Removal of all 18 specified identifiers (names, geographic subdivisions smaller than a State, dates directly related to the individual, telephone numbers, fax numbers, email addresses, SSNs, medical record numbers, health plan beneficiary numbers, account numbers, certificate/license numbers, vehicle identifiers, device identifiers, URLs, IP addresses, biometric identifiers, full-face photos, and any other unique identifying characteristic).

The SOP does not:

- Specify which de-identification method Meridian uses
- Identify the qualified expert for expert determinations
- Provide a checklist or procedure for the 18-identifier safe harbor
- Address documentation requirements for de-identification decisions
- Address the prohibition on re-identification or the treatment of re-identification codes (§ 164.502(d)(2))

---

#### 10. No De-Identification in the Context of Regulatory Filings

Given that Meridian's regulatory filings (e.g., SR 11-7 model reports, EU MDR PSURs, FDA submissions) may contain or be derived from PHI, the SOP should address whether and how PHI is de-identified before inclusion in regulatory submissions. This is entirely absent.

---

### C. STRUCTURAL / GOVERNANCE GAPS

#### 11. No HIPAA-Specific RFO for Breach Notification

The SOP assigns RFOs for SR 11-7 (MRO), GDPR (DPO), and security incidents (Deputy CISO), but no RFO is designated for HIPAA breach notification. The Security Incident Response Lead's scope is described in terms of "state breach notification laws and the EU AI Act" — HIPAA is conspicuously omitted.

#### 12. No HIPAA Breach Notification Template

Section 5.2 lists templates for GDPR breach notifications, SR 11-7 attestations, and EU MDR PSURs. No HIPAA breach notification template (individual, media, or HHS) is referenced.

---

### Summary Table

| # | Missing Element | HIPAA Citation | Severity |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Four-factor breach risk assessment | § 164.402(2) | **Critical** |
| 2 | Individual notification (timing, content, method) | § 164.404 | **Critical** |
| 3 | Media notification (>500 residents) | § 164.406 | **Critical** |
| 4 | HHS Secretary notification (500+/<500) | § 164.408 | **Critical** |
| 5 | Business Associate notification workflow | § 164.410 | **Critical** |
| 6 | Definition of "unsecured PHI" / encryption safe harbor | § 164.402 | **High** |
| 7 | Breach log for sub-500 incidents | § 164.408(c) | **High** |
| 8 | Law enforcement delay provision | § 164.412 | **Medium** |
| 9 | De-identification standard & procedure (expert determination or safe harbor) | § 164.514(a)–(b) | **Critical** |
| 10 | De-identification in regulatory filing context | § 164.514 | **High** |
| 11 | HIPAA-specific RFO for breach notification | Governance | **High** |
| 12 | HIPAA breach notification templates | Operational | **High** |

---

**Compliance level: gap**

The SOP-LEGC-005 is well-structured for SR 11-7, EU MDR, GDPR, and AI Act reporting obligations, but it contains a near-total gap with respect to HIPAA's breach notification and de-identification requirements. None of the core HIPAA Breach Notification Rule obligations (§§ 164.402–164.414) or the de-identification standard (§ 164.514) are addressed. This represents a material compliance exposure for a company named "Meridian Health Technologies" that handles PHI and lists HIPAA as an in-scope regulatory regime. Remediation should prioritize the four-factor risk assessment procedure, individual/HHS/media notification workflows, and the de-identification framework.

scores.judge_rationale: The candidate answer thoroughly identifies all missing elements from the reference (de-identification methodology, breach notification procedures/timelines/content, minimum-necessary policy, accounting of disclosures) and correctly cites all expected HIPAA sections (§164.404, §164.408, §164.502(b), §164.514(b), §164.528) plus additional relevant ones.

question.question: Reviewing SOP-AIML-006 (Model Performance Monitoring) against SR 11-7's ongoing monitoring expectations, what specific elements are MISSING?
question.expected_answer: Missing: (1) defined performance thresholds and back-testing cadence tied to model risk tier — monitoring is referenced but specific KPIs and review frequencies are not pinned; (2) challenger-model requirement for high-impact decisions — SR 11-7 expects benchmark/challenger comparisons; (3) escalation path from monitoring findings to senior management and the board (Section V governance); (4) overarching model-risk framework + model inventory anchoring the monitoring activities; (5) explicit linkage between drift signals and re-validation triggers.

expected_citations.section: Section III (Validation), Section IV (Ongoing Monitoring), Section V (Governance)
