## Gap Analysis: SOP-AIML-006 vs. SR 11-7 / SR 26-2 Ongoing Monitoring Expectations

SOP-AIML-006 is a well-structured document with strong operational monitoring (drift detection, alerting, playbooks). However, measured against SR 11-7's ongoing monitoring expectations — as clarified and expanded in the April 2026 SR 26-2 revision — the following **specific elements are MISSING**:

---

### 1. **Standalone Outcomes Analysis (Back-Testing & Benchmarking)**

**SR 11-7 / SR 26-2 Expectation:** SR 11-7 explicitly states that outcomes analysis can take the form of "standalone activities such as back-testing or outlier analysis" — not merely real-time metric dashboards. The guidance calls for comparing model outputs to corresponding real-world outcomes as a distinct, periodic exercise. SR 26-2 reinforces this, emphasizing "prediction-versus-actual comparisons" as a core ongoing monitoring activity.

**What's Missing in SOP-AIML-006:** The SOP relies entirely on real-time metric dashboards (Arize) and drift detection. There is **no provision for periodic, standalone back-testing** — i.e., systematically running historical data through the current model and comparing predictions against known outcomes. Section 5.2.2 addresses ground truth latency for delayed labels, but this is a pipeline for retroactive metric calculation, not a structured back-testing or outcomes analysis program. There is also **no benchmarking** of model performance against alternative models, simpler heuristics, or industry-standard benchmarks — a practice SR 11-7 identifies as a valid form of outcomes analysis.

---

### 2. **Model Limitations Assessment as Part of Ongoing Monitoring**

**SR 11-7 / SR 26-2 Expectation:** SR 11-7 states: "An effective ongoing monitoring plan may also include **regularly assessing any model limitations at the development stage and over time**, along with procedures for responding to any issues that may occur, before and after a model is approved for use." This is a forward-looking, qualitative assessment — not just quantitative metric tracking.

**What's Missing in SOP-AIML-006:** The SOP contains **no requirement to periodically reassess model limitations**. The onboarding process (Section 5.1) captures baseline metrics and data profiles, but there is no ongoing process to revisit the model's conceptual limitations, assumptions, or boundary conditions. For example, a clinical risk stratification model may have been developed with an explicit limitation that it was validated only on patients aged 18–65 — the SOP has no mechanism to periodically confirm this limitation remains documented and understood, or to assess whether the model is now being used outside that population.

---

### 3. **Model Materiality / Tiered Monitoring Framework**

**SR 11-7 / SR 26-2 Expectation:** SR 11-7 is explicit that "the frequency and scope of monitoring reports will depend on the nature of the model, the availability of new data or modeling approaches, and **model materiality**." SR 26-2 further defines model risk across four drivers: inherent risk, exposure, purpose, and use. Higher-materiality models warrant more comprehensive and rigorous oversight. The monitoring cadence should be risk-based, not uniform.

**What's Missing in SOP-AIML-006:** The SOP applies a **uniform monitoring approach** across all model categories. All models get the same 1-hour, 24-hour, and 7-day windows (Section 5.2), the same alert thresholds (Section 5.4.1), and the same reporting cadence (Section 7.2). While the SOP categorizes models by regulatory classification (High-Risk AI System, FDA 510(k), etc.), it does **not establish a materiality-tiered monitoring framework** that differentiates monitoring intensity, frequency, or depth based on model materiality. A clinical diagnostic imaging model and an operational triage bot receive essentially the same monitoring treatment.

---

### 4. **Independent / Objective Review of Ongoing Monitoring (Effective Challenge)**

**SR 11-7 / SR 26-2 Expectation:** SR 11-7 emphasizes "effective challenge" — "critical analysis conducted by **objective experts** who evaluate model risk and effect appropriate changes throughout the model lifecycle, from model development to **ongoing monitoring**." This requires individuals with "sufficient independence to maintain objectivity" and "organizational standing and influence to effect any change." SR 11-7 also addresses conflicts of interest between model development and validation groups.

**What's Missing in SOP-AIML-006:** The SOP assigns ongoing monitoring entirely to the MLOps team and Model Owners — the same individuals who developed or deploy the models. There is **no role for an independent validation function** in the ongoing monitoring process. The Model Owner (who may be the developer) leads root cause analysis (Section 5.5, Step 4). The Director of AI/ML reviews KPIs (Section 7.1). There is no provision for an independent model risk management or validation group to periodically review monitoring outputs, challenge the adequacy of thresholds, or assess whether monitoring practices remain appropriate. The SOP also makes **no mention of internal audit's role** in evaluating whether model risk management practices (including monitoring) are rigorous and effective — a specific SR 11-7 expectation.

---

### 5. **User Feedback Integration into Ongoing Monitoring**

**SR 11-7 / SR 26-2 Expectation:** SR 11-7 states: "Model use enables ongoing performance assessment as conditions evolve. **User feedback provides business insights** that enhance future development. Constructive engagement, particularly around model design, assumptions, and outcomes, strengthens both model understanding and model quality."

**What's Missing in SOP-AIML-006:** The SOP contains **no mechanism for capturing, structuring, or integrating user feedback** into the monitoring process. There is no feedback loop from model users (clinicians, financial analysts, operational staff) into the monitoring dashboards, alerting logic, or performance assessment. The degradation response playbook (Section 5.5) is entirely engineering-driven with no user-input channel.

---

### 6. **Model Overlays and Interim Adjustments**

**SR 11-7 / SR 26-2 Expectation:** SR 11-7 explicitly states: "A model that no longer performs as expected may warrant **overlays, adjustment, or redevelopment** of the model depending on a banking organization's model risk management policy as it pertains to model deterioration." Overlays are a distinct remediation category — temporary adjustments applied to model outputs while the underlying model issue is addressed.

**What's Missing in SOP-AIML-006:** The degradation response playbook (Section 5.5) lists three mitigation options: rollback, traffic shunt to a heuristic/fallback, or feature-off. There is **no provision for model overlays** — i.e., applying a calibrated adjustment to model outputs to compensate for known drift or deterioration while keeping the model in production. This is a significant gap for financial risk models where complete shutdown may be operationally disruptive.

---

### 7. **Aggregate Model Risk Monitoring**

**SR 11-7 / SR 26-2 Expectation:** SR 11-7 states: "Sound practice involves assessing model risk both individually and **in aggregate**. Aggregate risk reflects interactions and dependencies among models; reliance on common assumptions, data, or methodologies; and any other factors that could adversely affect several models and their outputs simultaneously."

**What's Missing in SOP-AIML-006:** The SOP monitors models individually. There is **no aggregate or portfolio-level monitoring** that identifies correlated drift across models sharing common features, data pipelines, or assumptions. For example, if the Meridian Feature Store experiences degradation, multiple models could drift simultaneously — the SOP has no mechanism to detect or escalate this as an aggregate risk event.

---

### 8. **Documentation of Monitoring Recommendations, Responses, and Exceptions Tracking**

**SR 11-7 / SR 26-2 Expectation:** SR 11-7 states: "Documentation can help maximize the likelihood of continuity of operations, including supporting the **tracking of recommendations, responses, and exceptions**; it can also be used to more effectively help manage any model remediation efforts."

**What's Missing in SOP-AIML-006:** While the SOP references post-mortems (Section 5.5, Step 5) and exception requests (Section 8.1), there is **no centralized tracking mechanism for monitoring-related recommendations** that emerge from incidents, RCA findings, or exception reviews. There is no requirement to maintain a living log of open recommendations, their status, and resolution. The exception process exists in ServiceNow but is not linked to an ongoing recommendations-tracking framework.

---

### 9. **Conditions Triggering Reassessment of Model Materiality**

**SR 11-7 / SR 26-2 Expectation:** SR 11-7 notes that for models deemed immaterial, "model risk management may consist of identifying those models and monitoring model performance and **conditions under which the use of those models may become material** to the banking organization in the future."

**What's Missing in SOP-AIML-006:** The SOP has no trigger mechanism to reassess whether a model's materiality classification has changed — e.g., due to expanded use, increased traffic volume, new regulatory requirements, or integration into higher-stakes workflows. A model initially classified as lower-risk could silently become material without any formal reassessment.

---

### Summary Table

| # | Missing Element | SR 11-7 / SR 26-2 Reference | Criticality |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Standalone back-testing / outcomes analysis | SR 11-7 §V (Outcomes Analysis); SR 26-2 | High |
| 2 | Periodic model limitations reassessment | SR 11-7 §V (Ongoing Model Monitoring) | High |
| 3 | Materiality-tiered monitoring framework | SR 11-7 §III, §V; SR 26-2 | High |
| 4 | Independent effective challenge of monitoring | SR 11-7 §III, §VI; SR 26-2 | High |
| 5 | User feedback integration | SR 11-7 §IV (Model Use) | Medium |
| 6 | Model overlays as remediation option | SR 11-7 §V (Ongoing Model Monitoring) | Medium |
| 7 | Aggregate / portfolio-level model risk monitoring | SR 11-7 §III | Medium |
| 8 | Recommendations & exceptions tracking framework | SR 11-7 §VI (Documentation) | Medium |
| 9 | Materiality reassessment triggers | SR 11-7 §III | Low-Medium |

---

**Compliance level: gap**

scores.judge_rationale: The candidate answer identifies several relevant gaps but is partially misaligned with the reference's five specific missing elements and relies heavily on SR 26-2 rather than the expected SR 11-7 sections, with incomplete citation mapping.

question.question: Reviewing SOP-CLIN-005 (Diagnostic AI Accuracy and Calibration) against NIST AI RMF's MEASURE function (including the GenAI Profile where relevant), what specific elements are MISSING?
question.expected_answer: Missing: (1) bias/fairness/equity testing methodology beyond accuracy — demographic-stratified performance metrics, calibration across protected groups (MEASURE 2/4); (2) AI risk-management framework anchoring measurement to risk tolerance (GOVERN); (3) AI system inventory linking the diagnostic AI to organisational risk (MAP 1); (4) MEASURE coverage of the seven trustworthiness characteristics — safety, security, accountability/transparency, explainability/interpretability, privacy, fair/bias — not just accuracy; (5) post-deployment monitoring closing the loop into MANAGE.
question.notes: Gap detection — clinical AI broader than accuracy.

output.answer:
I now have a thorough understanding of both the SOP and the NIST AI RMF MEASURE function (including the GenAI Profile). Let me compile the comprehensive gap analysis.

---

## Gap Analysis: SOP-CLIN-005 vs. NIST AI RMF MEASURE Function (incl. GenAI Profile)

SOP-CLIN-005 is a mature, well-structured document with strong coverage of accuracy, calibration, subgroup fairness, and post-market surveillance. However, when measured against the full NIST AI RMF MEASURE function (NIST AI 100-1, §5.3, Tables 3–4) and the GenAI Profile (NIST AI 600-1), the following specific elements are **missing or materially insufficient**:

---

### MEASURE 1: Appropriate Methods and Metrics

**1. MEASURE 1.1 — Documentation of risks/trustworthiness characteristics NOT measured.**
The SOP enumerates what *is* measured (ECE, AUC-ROC, sensitivity, specificity, PPV, NPV, calibration curves, data drift). However, it contains **no section documenting which trustworthiness characteristics or AI risks will not or cannot be measured**. NIST AI RMF 1.0 explicitly requires: *"The risks or trustworthiness characteristics that will not – or cannot – be measured are properly documented."* The SOP is silent on this. For example, there is no acknowledgment that causal validity, certain forms of concept drift, or long-term outcome equity may not be fully measurable with current methods.

**2. MEASURE 1.2 — Regular reassessment of metric appropriateness and control effectiveness, including error impact reports on affected communities.**
The SOP has monthly calibration audits (Section 6.2) and QBRs (Section 7.3), but these focus on *performance trends*, not on whether the metrics themselves remain appropriate. NIST requires: *"Appropriateness of AI metrics and effectiveness of existing controls are regularly assessed and updated, including reports of errors and potential impacts on affected communities."* There is no formal process to re-evaluate whether ECE remains the right calibration metric, whether the 0.05 threshold is still clinically appropriate, or to report error impacts specifically on affected patient communities.

**3. MEASURE 1.3 — Consultation with domain experts, external AI actors, and affected communities.**
The SOP's RACI matrix includes the Medical Advisory Board (external clinicians) and the Clinical Validation Lead, but there is **no mechanism for consulting affected patient communities or AI actors external to the development/deployment team** in regular assessments. NIST requires: *"Domain experts, users, AI actors external to the team that developed or deployed the AI system, and affected communities are consulted in support of assessments."* The SOP lacks patient/community advisory input into measurement design or assessment.

---

### MEASURE 2: Evaluation for Trustworthy Characteristics

**4. MEASURE 2.1 — Documentation of TEVV tools.**
The SOP mentions "Meridian Clinical ML Observability Platform (currently based on a customized MLflow and LangSmith stack)" (Section 5.3.2) but does **not document the specific tools, versions, configurations, or limitations** of the TEVV (Test, Evaluation, Verification, Validation) toolchain. NIST requires: *"Test sets, metrics, and details about the tools used during TEVV are documented."* The SOP lacks a formal TEVV tool documentation section.

**5. MEASURE 2.2 — Human subject protections and population representativeness.**
Section 5.5 addresses Human Factors evaluation in simulated clinical environments, but the SOP does **not explicitly address human subject protection requirements** (IRB, informed consent, data privacy for study participants) for evaluations involving human subjects. NIST requires: *"Evaluations involving human subjects meet applicable requirements (including human subject protection) and are representative of the relevant population."* The SOP also does not verify that the human subjects in validation studies are demographically representative of the target population.

**6. MEASURE 2.6 — Formal safety evaluation, residual risk tolerance, and "fail safely" beyond knowledge limits.**
The SOP has TC-02 (Confidence Thresholding) and TC-01 (Input Integrity Guard), which partially address failing safely. However, the SOP does **not** include:
- A formal, documented **safety risk evaluation** as a distinct activity (separate from accuracy/calibration testing).
- A defined **residual negative risk tolerance** threshold.
- Testing that the system **"can fail safely, particularly if made to operate beyond its knowledge limits"** — e.g., out-of-distribution detection testing, adversarial edge-case testing, or explicit boundary-condition stress testing. NIST requires: *"Safety metrics reflect system reliability and robustness, real-time monitoring, and response times for AI system failures."*

**7. MEASURE 2.7 — AI system security and resilience evaluation.**
The SOP has TC-04 (Adversarial Input Filter) but does **not include a comprehensive security and resilience evaluation process**. NIST requires: *"AI system security and resilience – as identified in the MAP function – are evaluated and documented."* Missing elements include: adversarial robustness testing (e.g., formal red-teaming), model extraction resistance testing, data poisoning resilience assessment, and documentation of security evaluation results. The GenAI Profile (MS-2.7 actions) further emphasizes red-teaming and security guardrail review.

**8. MEASURE 2.8 — Transparency and accountability risk examination.**
The SOP addresses transparency in Policy 4.5 (confidence scores in natural language) but does **not include a formal examination and documentation of risks associated with transparency and accountability**. NIST requires: *"Risks associated with transparency and accountability – as identified in the MAP function – are examined and documented."* Missing: documentation of what information is and is not disclosed to clinicians, patients, and regulators; accountability chain documentation for adverse outcomes; transparency about model limitations in the UI beyond the subgroup flags.

**9. MEASURE 2.9 — Model explanation, validation, and output interpretation documentation.**
The SOP requires confidence scores and calibration curves but does **not mandate explainability techniques** (e.g., SHAP, LIME, saliency maps, counterfactual explanations) for diagnostic AI models. NIST requires: *"The AI model is explained, validated, and documented, and AI system output is interpreted within its context."* The GenAI Profile (MS-2.9-001, MS-2.9-002) specifically requires documentation of ML explanation results, model architecture, optimization objectives, training algorithms, evaluation data, and ethical considerations — most of which are absent from the SOP.

**10. MEASURE 2.10 — Privacy risk examination.**
The SOP references HIPAA-compliant logging (TC-05) and hashed patient IDs, but does **not include a formal privacy risk examination**. NIST requires: *"Privacy risk of the AI system – as identified in the MAP function – is examined and documented."* Missing: membership inference attack risk assessment, model inversion risk, differential privacy considerations, training data privacy audit, and documentation of privacy risks.

**11. MEASURE 2.11 — Fairness and bias evaluation (beyond subgroup analysis).**
Section 5.8 (Subgroup Analysis) is strong but limited to pre-specified subgroups and performance metrics. NIST requires broader fairness evaluation. The GenAI Profile (MS-2.11-001 through MS-2.11-005) is particularly detailed, requiring:
- Fairness assessments measuring systemic bias across demographic groups using **both quality of service and allocation of services** metrics.
- **Field testing with sub-group populations** to determine likelihood of exposure to biased content.
- **AI red-teaming with counterfactual prompts**.
- General fairness metrics (demographic parity, equalized odds, equal opportunity) applied to pipeline outcomes.
- Measurement of **denigration prevalence** in outputs.
- Review of **proxy variables** for demographic group membership.
- Assessment of **synthetic vs. non-synthetic training data proportions** and homogeneity risks.

The SOP's subgroup analysis covers performance disparities but does not address these broader fairness measurement dimensions.

**12. MEASURE 2.12 — Environmental impact assessment.**
The SOP is **completely silent** on environmental impact. NIST requires: *"Environmental impact and sustainability of AI model training and management activities – as identified in the MAP function – are assessed and documented."* The GenAI Profile (MS-2.12-002, MS-2.12-003) requires documenting anticipated environmental impacts and measuring/estimating energy and water consumption for training, fine-tuning, and deployment.

**13. MEASURE 2.13 — TEVV metric and process effectiveness evaluation.**
The SOP does **not evaluate whether its TEVV metrics and processes are themselves effective**. NIST requires: *"Effectiveness of the employed TEVV metrics and processes in the MEASURE function are evaluated and documented."* The GenAI Profile (MS-2.13-001) specifically requires creating measurement error models for pre-deployment metrics to demonstrate construct validity — i.e., does ECE effectively operationalize calibration quality? Are the bootstrapped CIs valid? This meta-evaluation is absent.

---

### MEASURE 3: Risk Tracking Over Time

**14. MEASURE 3.1 — Regular identification and tracking of existing, unanticipated, and emergent AI risks.**
The SOP has strong post-market surveillance (Section 7.2) and drift monitoring (TC-03), but these focus on *known* risks (accuracy degradation, data drift). There is **no structured process for identifying unanticipated or emergent risks** — e.g., novel failure modes discovered in deployment, unexpected clinical workflows, or emergent biases. NIST requires: *"Approaches, personnel, and documentation are in place to regularly identify and track existing, unanticipated, and emergent AI risks based on factors such as intended and actual performance in deployed contexts."*

**15. MEASURE 3.2 — Risk tracking for hard-to-measure risks.**
The SOP does **not address risks that are difficult to assess with currently available measurement techniques**. NIST requires: *"Risk tracking approaches are considered for settings where AI risks are difficult to assess using currently available measurement techniques or where metrics are not yet available."* Examples for diagnostic AI: long-term downstream clinical outcomes, cognitive automation bias effects on clinician decision quality, or subtle diagnostic pathway shifts.

**16. MEASURE 3.3 — End-user and impacted community feedback integration into evaluation metrics.**
The SOP's "AI Concordance (Agree/Disagree)" field (Section 5.5.2) captures clinician feedback, but there is **no formal feedback process for end users (clinicians) or impacted communities (patients) to report problems or appeal system outcomes** that is integrated into AI system evaluation metrics. NIST requires: *"Feedback processes for end users and impacted communities to report problems and appeal system outcomes are established and integrated into AI system evaluation metrics."*

---

### MEASURE 4: Feedback About Measurement Efficacy

**17. MEASURE 4.1 — Measurement approaches connected to deployment context via domain expert consultation.**
The SOP's measurement design (Section 5.1, Clinical Performance Protocol) is developed by the Lead Data Scientist and Clinical Validation Lead, but there is **no documented process for connecting measurement approaches to deployment context through consultation with domain experts and end users**. NIST requires this consultation and its documentation.

**18. MEASURE 4.2 — Validation that the system performs consistently as intended, informed by domain experts and AI actors.**
While the SOP has independent clinical validation (Section 5.4), it does **not include a process for domain experts and relevant AI actors to validate whether the system is performing consistently as intended across deployment contexts**. The validation is statistical, not contextual/qualitative.

**19. MEASURE 4.3 — Identification and documentation of measurable performance improvements or declines based on consultations and field data.**
The SOP's QBR (Section 7.3) reports performance trends but does **not systematically identify and document measurable improvements or declines based on consultations with relevant AI actors, including affected communities, and field data about context-relevant risks and trustworthiness characteristics**. NIST requires this specifically.

---

### GenAI Profile — Specific Additional Gaps

If any of Meridian's diagnostic AI systems incorporate generative AI components (e.g., LLM-based clinical summarization, generative image enhancement, or report generation), the following GenAI Profile MEASURE actions are additionally missing:

**20. MS-2.3-003/004 — Pre-deployment testing results sharing and purpose-built testing environments.** No requirement to share pre-deployment testing results with relevant GAI actors or use purpose-built testing environments (e.g., NIST Dioptra).

**21. MS-2.5-003 — Review and verification of sources/citations in system outputs.** For any generative component producing clinical text, no requirement to verify sources and citations.

**22. MS-2.5-004 — Tracking anthropomorphization in interfaces.** No requirement to track or document anthropomorphization in AI system interfaces.

**23. MS-2.5-005 — Training data and TEVV data provenance verification.** No requirement to verify that fine-tuning or RAG data is grounded and provenance-verified.

**24. MS-2.8-003/004 — Content provenance and user instruction adequacy.** No content provenance tracking (C2PA, watermarking) or verification of user instruction adequacy through testing.

**25. MS-2.11-004 — Comprehensive bias source review in training/TEVV data.** No requirement to review differences in outcome distributions across intersecting groups, completeness/representativeness of data sources, latent systemic bias in unstructured data, proxy features, digital divide impacts, or prevalence of GAI-generated data in training data.

---

### Summary Table of Missing Elements

| # | NIST AI RMF Reference | Missing Element | Severity |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | MEASURE 1.1 | Documentation of risks/characteristics NOT measured | High |
| 2 | MEASURE 1.2 | Regular reassessment of metric appropriateness + community impact reports | High |
| 3 | MEASURE 1.3 | Consultation with affected communities and external AI actors | Medium |
| 4 | MEASURE 2.1 | Formal TEVV tool documentation (versions, configs, limitations) | Medium |
| 5 | MEASURE 2.2 | Human subject protections + population representativeness verification | High |
| 6 | MEASURE 2.6 | Formal safety evaluation, residual risk tolerance, fail-safely-beyond-knowledge-limits testing | **Critical** |
| 7 | MEASURE 2.7 | Comprehensive security/resilience evaluation (red-teaming, extraction resistance, poisoning) | High |
| 8 | MEASURE 2.8 | Formal transparency and accountability risk examination and documentation | High |
| 9 | MEASURE 2.9 | Model explainability techniques mandate + model documentation (architecture, objectives, etc.) | High |
| 10 | MEASURE 2.10 | Formal privacy risk examination (membership inference, model inversion, differential privacy) | High |
| 11 | MEASURE 2.11 | Broader fairness evaluation beyond subgroup performance (systemic bias, red-teaming, proxy review) | High |
| 12 | MEASURE 2.12 | Environmental impact assessment (energy, water, carbon) | Medium |
| 13 | MEASURE 2.13 | TEVV metric/process effectiveness meta-evaluation (measurement error models, construct validity) | High |
| 14 | MEASURE 3.1 | Structured process for identifying unanticipated/emergent risks | High |
| 15 | MEASURE 3.2 | Risk tracking for hard-to-measure risks | Medium |
| 16 | MEASURE 3.3 | End-user/community feedback integrated into evaluation metrics (problem reporting, appeals) | High |
| 17 | MEASURE 4.1 | Deployment-context measurement consultation with domain experts, documented | Medium |
| 18 | MEASURE 4.2 | Domain expert validation of consistent performance across deployment contexts | Medium |
| 19 | MEASURE 4.3 | Systematic identification of improvements/declines from consultations and field data | Medium |
| 20–25 | GenAI Profile (various) | Pre-deployment testing sharing, output verification, anthropomorphization tracking, data provenance, content provenance, comprehensive bias source review | Conditional* |

*\*Conditional on whether generative AI components are used in diagnostic systems.*

---

### Conclusion

SOP-CLIN-005 is strong on **statistical performance measurement** (accuracy, calibration, drift) and has commendable subgroup fairness analysis and post-market surveillance. However, it was clearly designed primarily against medical device standards (ISO 13485, ISO 14971, IEC 62304, GMLP) rather than the NIST AI RMF. The most critical gaps are: **(a)** the absence of a formal safety evaluation with defined residual risk tolerance and fail-safely-beyond-knowledge-limits testing (MEASURE 2.6), **(b)** the absence of a Quality Management System aligned with AI-specific requirements (MEASURE embedded in Article 17), **(c)** the lack of comprehensive security/resilience, privacy, and explainability evaluations (MEASURE 2.7, 2.9, 2.10), and **(d)** the absence of structured processes for emergent risk identification, community feedback integration, and TEVV meta-evaluation (MEASURE 3.1, 3.3, 2.13, and all of MEASURE 4).

**Compliance level: PARTIAL — significant gaps exist.**

Compliance level: **PARTIAL**

scores.judge_rationale: The candidate answer comprehensively identifies all missing elements from the reference (bias/fairness beyond accuracy, risk tolerance anchoring, system inventory, and coverage of all trustworthiness characteristics) and correctly cites GOVERN, MAP 1, and MEASURE 2/3/4, while also providing extensive additional detail aligned with the NIST AI RMF MEASURE function.

question.question: Reviewing SOP-AIML-009 (Algorithmic Bias Detection and Mitigation) against GDPR Article 35 (DPIA) and Article 22 (automated decision-making), what specific procedures are MISSING?
question.expected_answer: Missing: (1) Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA) procedure under Article 35 — systematic description of processing, necessity/proportionality assessment, risks-to-rights evaluation, measures envisaged; (2) prior-consultation procedure with the supervisory authority (Article 36) when residual risk remains high; (3) Article 22 procedures for solely-automated significant-effect decisions — meaningful information about the logic involved, right to obtain human intervention, right to express the data subject's point of view, right to contest the decision; (4) Articles 13/14 specificity in privacy notices regarding the bias-detection processing and its purposes.
question.notes: Gap detection across multiple GDPR articles for a bias SOP.

output.answer:
Now I have a comprehensive picture. Let me compile the full gap analysis.

---

## Gap Analysis: SOP-AIML-009 vs. GDPR Article 35 (DPIA) and Article 22 (Automated Decision-Making)

### Overview

SOP-AIML-009 is a mature, well-structured internal governance document for algorithmic bias. However, when measured against the specific legal requirements of GDPR Articles 35 and 22, there are **significant procedural gaps** — particularly in the areas where the SOP's internal risk-management framework does not map to the GDPR's mandatory legal obligations. Below is the detailed missing-procedure analysis.

---

## MISSING PROCEDURES — Article 35 (DPIA)

### 1. No Procedure for Triggering a DPIA Based on Article 35(3) Criteria

**What GDPR requires:** Article 35(3)(a) mandates a DPIA for "a systematic and extensive evaluation of personal aspects relating to natural persons which is based on automated processing, including profiling, and on which decisions are based that produce legal effects concerning the natural person or similarly significantly affect the natural person." Article 35(3)(b) also triggers a DPIA for large-scale processing of special categories of data (Article 9), which includes health data.

**What SOP-AIML-009 contains:** The SOP references "SOP-PRI-008 — Conducting a Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA)" in Section 10.1 and mentions that the EU DPO reviews DPIAs "where algorithmic bias creates a high risk to individuals" (Section 3). Section 5.1, Step 5 requires a Privacy Consultation Ticket (category `Bias-Testing-DPIA`) when PII/PHI is involved in bias testing.

**What is MISSING:** There is no procedure within SOP-AIML-009 itself that:
- **Systematically determines when a DPIA is mandatory** under Article 35(3). The SOP defers entirely to SOP-PRI-008, but never establishes a cross-reference trigger. For example, the Clinical AI Platform's risk scoring and HealthPay's lending models both involve "systematic and extensive evaluation" producing "legal effects or similarly significant effects" — yet the SOP contains no step that flags these as DPIA-triggering.
- **Integrates DPIA findings into the bias testing workflow.** The DPIA under Article 35(7) must contain: (a) a systematic description of processing, (b) necessity and proportionality assessment, (c) risk assessment, and (d) measures to address risks. The SOP's `BiasComplianceCheck` (Section 5.4.2) does not verify that a DPIA has been completed or that its risk mitigation measures have been incorporated.

### 2. No Procedure for Necessity and Proportionality Assessment (Article 35(7)(b))

**What GDPR requires:** Article 35(7)(b) requires "an assessment of the necessity and proportionality of the processing operations in relation to the purposes."

**What SOP-AIML-009 contains:** The SOP has a remediation hierarchy (Section 5.5) that addresses bias mitigation trade-offs, and the IRB adjudicates "fairness vs. accuracy trade-offs" (Section 6.2, A-02). But these are fairness-centric, not data-protection-centric.

**What is MISSING:** There is no procedure that asks: *Is the processing of personal data (including special category data) for this model's bias detection and mitigation actually necessary and proportionate?* For example:
- The proxy detection module (Section 5.2.2) processes attributes correlated with protected characteristics — but there is no necessity assessment documenting *why* processing race-correlated proxies is indispensable.
- The stratified splitting (Section 5.2.4) and sub-group performance metrics (Section 5.3.2) require processing data segmented by protected attributes. No proportionality analysis justifies this against the intrusion on data subject privacy.

### 3. No Procedure for Seeking Data Subject Views (Article 35(9))

**What GDPR requires:** Article 35(9): "Where appropriate, the controller shall seek the views of data subjects or their representatives on the intended processing, without prejudice to the protection of commercial or public interests or the security of processing operations."

**What SOP-AIML-009 contains:** The SOP's stakeholder matrix (Section 3) includes Product Managers, Data Scientists, Compliance, Legal, the IRB, and the EU DPO — but **no data subjects or their representatives**. The IRB (Section 3) comprises the CAIO, VP of Engineering, Chief Compliance Officer, and a rotating Product lead — all internal Meridian personnel.

**What is MISSING:** There is no procedure whatsoever for:
- Identifying when data subject consultation is "appropriate" (e.g., for Critical-risk clinical models making consequential health decisions about EU data subjects).
- A mechanism to seek patient/customer views (e.g., patient advocacy groups, customer panels, or worker representatives for employment-related models).
- Documenting how those views were considered or why consultation was deemed inappropriate.

### 4. No Procedure for Prior Consultation with Supervisory Authority (Article 36)

**What GDPR requires:** Article 36(1): "The controller shall consult the supervisory authority prior to processing where a data protection impact assessment under Article 35 indicates that the processing would result in a high risk in the absence of measures taken by the controller to mitigate the risk."

**What SOP-AIML-009 contains:** The SOP has an escalation path (Section 8.3) and an exception handling procedure (Section 8.2), but these are internal governance mechanisms. The `BiasComplianceCheck` (Section 5.4.2) gates deployment on internal approvals only.

**What is MISSING:** There is no procedure that:
- Evaluates whether residual bias risk after mitigation constitutes a "high risk" under Article 36 that requires consultation with the competent supervisory authority (e.g., the Berlin DPA for Meridian's Berlin office, or the Irish DPC if Berlin is not the lead authority).
- Triggers a halt to deployment pending supervisory authority feedback (the Article 36 timeline of up to 8 weeks + 6 week extension).
- Documents the Article 36 consultation outcome and integrates it into the deployment approval workflow.

### 5. No Procedure for DPIA Review Triggered by Model/Data Changes (Article 35(11))

**What GDPR requires:** Article 35(11): "Where necessary, the controller shall carry out a review to assess if processing is performed in accordance with the data protection impact assessment at least when there is a change of the risk represented by processing operations."

**What SOP-AIML-009 contains:** Section 5.6.3 requires periodic re-assessment of bias (every 180 days for Standard, every 30 days for High/Critical). Section 5.6.2 triggers a P0 incident for bias drift >20%.

**What is MISSING:** There is no procedure linking bias drift or model retraining to a **mandatory DPIA review**. If a model is retrained with new data, or if bias drift is detected that changes the risk profile to data subjects, Article 35(11) requires revisiting the DPIA. The SOP's monitoring framework (Section 5.6) is entirely fairness-metric-driven and does not incorporate a DPIA review trigger.

---

## MISSING PROCEDURES — Article 22 (Automated Decision-Making)

### 6. No Procedure for Identifying "Solely Automated" Decisions Producing Legal/Similarly Significant Effects (Article 22(1))

**What GDPR requires:** Article 22(1): "The data subject shall have the right not to be subject to a decision based solely on automated processing, including profiling, which produces legal effects concerning him or her or similarly significantly affects him or her."

**What SOP-AIML-009 contains:** Section 4.3 ("Human-in-the-Loop for High-Risk Decisions") states that no Critical or High-risk model "shall make a fully automated, consequential decision about an individual without meaningful human oversight." This applies to the Clinical AI Platform and HealthPay patient financing. The SOP references "SOP-AIML-022 — Human-in-the-Loop Mandate for Critical Systems" (Section 10.1).

**What is MISSING:** The SOP does not contain a procedure to:
- **Classify each model decision as "solely automated" or "human-reviewed"** for Article 22 purposes. The human-in-the-loop policy (Section 4.3) is risk-tier-based (Critical/High), not decision-effect-based. A "Standard" risk model could still produce "similarly significant effects" (e.g., a MedInsight care-gap model that deprioritizes certain patients) and fall under Article 22.
- **Determine whether a decision produces "legal effects" or "similarly significant effects."** The SOP's Model Risk Tier classification (Section 2, "Model Risk Tier") is referenced but never cross-walked to the Article 22 legal-effects threshold. HealthPay lending decisions clearly produce legal effects; Clinical risk scores may "similarly significantly affect" individuals — but no procedure systematically makes this determination.

### 7. No Procedure for Securing a Valid Article 22(2) Exception Basis

**What GDPR requires:** Article 22(2) permits solely automated decisions only if: (a) necessary for a contract, (b) authorized by Union/Member State law, or (c) based on explicit consent.

**What SOP-AIML-009 contains:** The SOP's lawful basis for processing PII/PHI in bias testing is addressed via the Privacy Consultation Ticket (Section 5.1, Step 5), which confirms "the lawful basis for processing." But this is about the *bias testing activity*, not the *underlying automated decision*.

**What is MISSING:** There is no procedure that:
- **For each production model making automated decisions about EU data subjects, identifies and documents which Article 22(2) exception applies** (contractual necessity, legal authorization, or explicit consent).
- **For models relying on explicit consent (Article 22(2)(c)), establishes a consent collection, management, and withdrawal mechanism.** The SOP's scope covers HealthPay lending models — if these rely on consent, there is no procedure for obtaining, recording, or honoring withdrawal of that consent.
- **For models relying on contractual necessity (Article 22(2)(a)), documents the necessity analysis** demonstrating that the automated decision is genuinely indispensable to the contract.

### 8. No Procedure for Providing "Meaningful Information About the Logic Involved" (Articles 13(2)(f), 14(2)(g), 15(1)(h))

**What GDPR requires:** Articles 13(2)(f), 14(2)(g), and 15(1)(h) require that data subjects be provided with "the existence of automated decision-making, including profiling, referred to in Article 22(1) and (4) and, at least in those cases, meaningful information about the logic involved, as well as the significance and the envisaged consequences of such processing for the data subject."

**What SOP-AIML-009 contains:** Section 4.5 mandates Model Cards with "intended use, performance metrics across sub-groups, and known limitations." Section 4.3 requires that human reviewers have access to an "explainability dashboard." These are internal transparency mechanisms.

**What is MISSING:** There is no procedure for:
- **Translating Model Card content into externally facing, "meaningful information about the logic involved"** in clear, plain language as required by Article 12(1). Model Cards are internal MLflow artifacts — there is no procedure to extract and communicate the logic, significance, and consequences to the data subject in a privacy notice or at the point of decision.
- **Proactively notifying data subjects** at the point of data collection (Article 13) or within a reasonable period (Article 14) that automated decision-making is occurring, what logic is used, and what the consequences are.
- **Responding to Article 15 access requests** that specifically ask for "meaningful information about the logic involved." The SOP has no interface with Meridian's Data Subject Access Request (DSAR) handling process.

### 9. No Procedure for Data Subjects to "Express Their Point of View and Contest the Decision" (Article 22(3))

**What GDPR requires:** Article 22(3): "the data controller shall implement suitable measures to safeguard the data subject's rights and freedoms and legitimate interests, at least the right to obtain human intervention on the part of the controller, to express his or her point of view and to contest the decision."

**What SOP-AIML-009 contains:** Section 4.3 provides for human-in-the-loop oversight where "the human reviewer must have the authority and competence to override the algorithmic recommendation." This is an *internal operational control*, not a *data subject right*.

**What is MISSING:** There is no procedure for:
- **A data subject to initiate a contestation of an automated decision.** The SOP has no intake mechanism (e.g., a dedicated channel, form, or process) through which an EU data subject can challenge an algorithmic decision made about them.
- **Routing a contested decision to a human reviewer with the authority to override it.** Section 4.3's human-in-the-loop is a pre-deployment design requirement, not a post-decision remedy mechanism. The SOP does not describe what happens when a patient contests a Clinical AI Platform risk score or a customer challenges a HealthPay lending denial.
- **Documenting the outcome of a contestation** and feeding it back into model improvement. There is no feedback loop from contested decisions to the bias monitoring or retraining pipeline.
- **Timelines for responding to contestations.** Article 12(3) requires responses "without undue delay and in any event within one month." The SOP is silent on SLA for human review of contested automated decisions.

### 10. No Procedure Addressing the Article 22(4) Prohibition on Special Category Data

**What GDPR requires:** Article 22(4): "Decisions referred to in paragraph 2 shall not be based on special categories of personal data referred to in Article 9(1), unless point (a) or (g) of Article 9(2) applies and suitable measures to safeguard the data subject's rights and freedoms and legitimate interests are in place."

**What SOP-AIML-009 contains:** The SOP's scope covers the Clinical AI Platform (risk scoring, diagnostic imaging, adverse event prediction) — all of which process "data concerning health" (Article 9(1)). Section 4.4 addresses data minimization for bias testing. Section 5.2.2 addresses proxy detection.

**What is MISSING:** There is no procedure that:
- **For each model making automated decisions based on special category data (health data, genetic data, etc.), identifies which Article 9(2) exception applies** (explicit consent under Article 9(2)(a) or substantial public interest under Article 9(2)(g)).
- **Documents the "suitable measures to safeguard the data subject's rights and freedoms and legitimate interests"** as required by the Article 22(4) chapeau. The SOP's fairness metrics and bias thresholds are technical safeguards, but they are not framed or documented as Article 22(4) "suitable measures."
- **Addresses the interaction between bias testing (which requires processing protected attributes) and the Article 9 prohibition.** The SOP's proxy detection (Section 5.2.2) and sub-group analysis (Section 5.3.2) inherently involve processing data that reveals or correlates with special category data — yet there is no procedure confirming that an Article 9(2) exception covers this specific processing purpose.

---

## Summary Table of Missing Procedures

| # | Missing Procedure | GDPR Article(s) | Criticality |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Systematic DPIA trigger determination for AI/ML models | Art. 35(1), 35(3) | **High** |
| 2 | Necessity & proportionality assessment for bias-related data processing | Art. 35(7)(b) | **High** |
| 3 | Data subject / representative consultation on intended processing | Art. 35(9) | **Medium** |
| 4 | Prior consultation with supervisory authority when residual high risk | Art. 36(1) | **High** |
| 5 | DPIA review trigger upon model change / bias drift | Art. 35(11) | **Medium** |
| 6 | Classification of decisions as "solely automated" with legal/significant effects | Art. 22(1) | **Critical** |
| 7 | Identification and documentation of Article 22(2) lawful exception basis | Art. 22(2) | **Critical** |
| 8 | External-facing "meaningful information about the logic involved" procedure | Arts. 13(2)(f), 14(2)(g), 15(1)(h) | **Critical** |
| 9 | Data subject right to contest automated decisions and obtain human review | Art. 22(3) | **Critical** |
| 10 | Article 9(2) exception basis and suitable measures for special category data in ADM | Art. 22(4) r/w Art. 9 | **Critical** |

---

## Conclusion

SOP-AIML-009 is a technically robust bias governance framework, but it operates primarily as an **internal model risk management instrument**. It lacks the **externally-facing, rights-based procedural infrastructure** that GDPR Articles 35 and 22 demand. The most critical gaps are: (1) the absence of a data subject contestation mechanism (Article 22(3)), (2) the absence of external transparency procedures for meaningful logic disclosure (Articles 13–15), (3) the absence of a systematic Article 22(2) lawful-basis determination for each automated-decision model, and (4) the absence of DPIA integration and Article 36 prior consultation triggers within the bias governance workflow. These are not merely documentation gaps — they represent missing operational procedures that, if unaddressed, expose Meridian Health Technologies to regulatory enforcement risk across its EU establishments (London, Berlin) and for any processing of EU data subjects.

**Compliance rating: PARTIAL — Significant gaps exist.**

Compliance level: **Partial**

scores.judge_rationale: The candidate answer comprehensively identifies all missing procedures from the reference (DPIA, prior consultation, Article 22 rights, Articles 13/14 notices) and correctly cites all expected GDPR articles (13, 14, 22, 35, 36) with precise section references.

question.question: Reviewing SOP-PENG-001 (Secure Software Development Lifecycle) against EU AI Act Article 9 (risk management system), what specific elements are MISSING for an SDLC supporting high-risk AI systems?
question.expected_answer: Missing: (1) continuous lifecycle risk-management process (Article 9 §2) — identification/analysis/estimation/evaluation of risks throughout development and after deployment, integrated into the SDLC phases; (2) elimination or reduction of identified risks through design and development decisions (Article 9 §4); (3) testing procedures explicitly verifying high-risk AI requirements against acceptable residual risk thresholds (Article 9 §6, §7); (4) consideration of foreseeable misuse and the use environment in risk assessment (Article 9 §3); (5) linkage to post-market-monitoring data feeding back into the SDLC (Article 9 §2 lifecycle loop).
question.notes: Gap detection — SDLC needs Article 9 lifecycle risk management baked in.

output.answer:
I now have a comprehensive picture. Let me compile the gap analysis.

---

## Gap Analysis: SOP-PENG-001 vs. EU AI Act Article 9 — Risk Management System for High-Risk AI Systems

### Overview

SOP-PENG-001 is a mature, well-structured Secure SDLC that addresses HIPAA, SOC 2, and general software security concerns. However, when measured against **Article 9 of the EU AI Act** (as finalized, with enforcement from 2 August 2026), the SOP has **significant structural gaps** that would prevent it from supporting high-risk AI system development in compliance with the Regulation. Below are the specific missing elements, organized by Article 9's requirements.

---

### 1. MISSING: Risk Identification Scoped to Health, Safety, and Fundamental Rights (Art. 9(2)(a))

**What Article 9 requires:** The risk management system must identify and analyze "the known and the reasonably foreseeable risks that the high-risk AI system can pose to **health, safety or fundamental rights** when the high-risk AI system is used in accordance with its intended purpose."

**What SOP-PENG-001 provides:** The SOP's threat modeling (Section 5.2.2) uses the **STRIDE** methodology, which is an information-security-centric framework. STRIDE identifies threats to confidentiality, integrity, and availability — not risks to health, safety, or fundamental rights (e.g., discrimination, bias, denial of services, privacy harms beyond data breach). The Threat Model Document (TMD) template captures STRIDE categories, affected components, and a 5×5 risk matrix — but there is **no requirement to identify or analyze risks to fundamental rights** such as non-discrimination (Article 21 CFR), human dignity, or access to healthcare.

**Gap:** The SOP lacks any methodology or requirement for identifying AI-specific harms — algorithmic bias, unfair treatment, erroneous clinical predictions, or safety risks from model errors. STRIDE alone is insufficient.

---

### 2. MISSING: Risk Evaluation Under Conditions of Reasonably Foreseeable Misuse (Art. 9(2)(b))

**What Article 9 requires:** Risks must be estimated and evaluated "when the high-risk AI system is used in accordance with its intended purpose, **and under conditions of reasonably foreseeable misuse**."

**What SOP-PENG-001 provides:** Section 5.2.2 mentions "Abuser Stories" capturing misuse cases, which is a partial nod in this direction. However, these are framed as traditional security abuse cases (brute-force, IDOR, injection). There is **no requirement to model AI-specific misuse**: e.g., a clinician over-relying on an AI diagnostic output, adversarial inputs designed to fool an ML model, or use of the system on populations for which it was not validated.

**Gap:** The SOP does not require a structured analysis of reasonably foreseeable misuse of AI/ML systems specifically — such as concept drift exploitation, data poisoning, or off-label deployment scenarios.

---

### 3. MISSING: Post-Market Monitoring Integration into Risk Management (Art. 9(2)(c))

**What Article 9 requires:** The risk management system must evaluate "other risks possibly arising, based on the analysis of data gathered from the **post-market monitoring system** referred to in Article 72." Article 72 requires a documented post-market monitoring plan that "actively and systematically collect[s], document[s] and analyse[s] relevant data… on the performance of high-risk AI systems throughout their lifetime."

**What SOP-PENG-001 provides:** Section 5.6 (Operations / Post-Deployment) covers continuous vulnerability scanning (Wiz, Snyk), dependency monitoring, and bug bounty intake. Section 7 covers KPIs like MTTR for CVEs. However, there is **no post-market monitoring plan** for AI/ML model performance — no requirement to monitor for model drift, accuracy degradation, fairness metrics over time, or emergent behaviors in production. The monitoring described is purely infrastructure and vulnerability-focused, not AI-performance-focused.

**Gap:** The SOP has no AI-specific post-market monitoring plan. There is no feedback loop from production model performance data back into the risk management system, as required by Art. 9(2)(c) read with Art. 72.

---

### 4. MISSING: Risk Management Measures Hierarchy and Residual Risk Determination (Art. 9(4)-(5))

**What Article 9 requires:** Risk management measures must follow a specific hierarchy: (a) elimination or reduction of risks "as far as technically feasible through adequate design and development"; (b) mitigation and control measures for risks that cannot be eliminated; (c) provision of information under Article 13 and training to deployers. The **overall residual risk** must be "judged to be acceptable" and **communicated to the user**.

**What SOP-PENG-001 provides:** The SOP's threat modeling (Section 5.2.2) captures inherent risk, planned mitigations, and residual risk in the TMD. However, there is no explicit requirement to apply the **design-first elimination hierarchy** mandated by Art. 9(4)-(5). The SOP does not require a formal determination that overall residual AI risk is "acceptable" before deployment, nor does it require residual risks to be communicated to the deployer/user.

**Gap:** No mandated risk elimination hierarchy. No formal residual risk acceptability determination. No requirement to communicate residual AI risks to deployers.

---

### 5. MISSING: AI-Specific Testing with Pre-Defined Metrics and Probabilistic Thresholds (Art. 9(5)-(8))

**What Article 9 requires:** High-risk AI systems "shall be tested for the purposes of identifying the most appropriate risk management measures." Testing must ensure "consistent performance for their intended purpose." Critically, Art. 9(8) requires testing "against **prior defined metrics and probabilistic thresholds** that are appropriate to the intended purpose of the high-risk AI system." Testing must occur "at any time throughout the development process, and, in any event, prior to their being placed on the market or put into service."

**What SOP-PENG-001 provides:** The SOP's testing phase (Section 5.4) covers functional security testing (auth bypass, injection, XSS, rate limiting), DAST (OWASP ZAP), and annual penetration testing. There is **no requirement for AI/ML model-specific testing** — no requirement to pre-define accuracy metrics, fairness metrics, robustness thresholds, or probabilistic performance criteria. There is no requirement to test for model consistency across relevant subpopulations or under distribution shift.

**Gap:** The SOP lacks any AI/ML model testing framework with pre-defined metrics and probabilistic thresholds. The testing phase is entirely security-focused and does not address model performance, fairness, or robustness validation.

---

### 6. MISSING: Consideration of Vulnerable Groups Including Children (Art. 9(9))

**What Article 9 requires:** "When implementing the risk management system… providers shall give consideration to whether in view of its intended purpose the high-risk AI system is likely to have an adverse impact on **persons under the age of 18** and, as appropriate, **other vulnerable groups**."

**What SOP-PENG-001 provides:** The SOP mentions "Privacy Requirements" in Section 5.1 (Step 4) referencing GDPR/CCPA, and the HIPAA controls address PHI protection. However, there is **no requirement anywhere in the SOP** to assess whether an AI system may have disproportionate adverse impacts on children, elderly patients, persons with disabilities, economically disadvantaged populations, or other vulnerable groups.

**Gap:** No requirement to assess adverse impacts on children or vulnerable groups as part of risk management — a specific, non-delegable obligation under Art. 9(9).

---

### 7. MISSING: Integration with Article 13 Transparency Obligations (Art. 9(4)(c))

**What Article 9 requires:** Risk management measures must include "provision of information required pursuant to Article 13 and, where appropriate, training to deployers." Article 13 requires high-risk AI systems to be designed with transparency — including information about the system's intended purpose, level of accuracy, known limitations, and risks to health, safety, and fundamental rights.

**What SOP-PENG-001 provides:** The SOP references training for internal personnel (Section 9) and documentation (PRD, TMD, design review checklists). However, there is **no requirement to produce deployer-facing transparency documentation** — no instructions for use, no disclosure of accuracy/limitations, no communication of residual risks to the end-user organization.

**Gap:** No process for generating Article 13-compliant transparency information for deployers. No linkage between risk management outputs and user-facing documentation.

---

### 8. MISSING: Continuous Iterative Lifecycle Process with Regular Systematic Updating (Art. 9(2))

**What Article 9 requires:** The risk management system must be "a **continuous iterative process** planned and run throughout the **entire lifecycle** of a high-risk AI system, requiring **regular systematic review and updating**."

**What SOP-PENG-001 provides:** The SOP covers phases from Requirements through Decommissioning (Phases 1-7), and Section 5.2.2 requires threat modeling "at least annually for all Tier-1 applications." However, the risk management process is **phase-gated, not continuous**. Once a TMD is approved at Design, there is no requirement to iteratively revisit and update it based on operational data, post-market findings, or model behavior changes. The annual review cadence is not tied to AI-specific triggers (e.g., data drift detection, new subpopulation deployment).

**Gap:** The SOP's risk management is gate-based, not continuous-iterative. No trigger-based review mechanism linked to AI system performance data.

---

### 9. MISSING: Documentation of the Risk Management System Itself (Art. 9(1))

**What Article 9 requires:** "A risk management system shall be **established, implemented, documented and maintained** in relation to high-risk AI systems."

**What SOP-PENG-001 provides:** The SOP documents the SSDLC process, but it does not itself constitute or document a "risk management system" as defined by Article 9. There is no single, coherent document or system description that covers the four-step cycle (identify → evaluate → post-market → mitigate) required by Art. 9(2). The risk management activities are fragmented across threat modeling (5.2.2), vulnerability management (5.6), and the exception process (Section 8).

**Gap:** No unified, documented risk management system description that maps to the Art. 9(2) cycle. The SOP would need a dedicated AI Risk Management System annex or companion document.

---

### 10. MISSING: Serious Incident Reporting Integration (Art. 73)

**What Article 9 implies (read with Art. 73):** Providers must report any "serious incident" to market surveillance authorities. A serious incident includes any incident that "directly or indirectly leads, might have led or might lead to… the death of a person or serious damage to a person's health."

**What SOP-PENG-001 provides:** Section 5.6 references the bug bounty/VDP and vulnerability management. SOP-ITOP-001 (Incident Response) is referenced. However, there is **no mention of AI-specific serious incident reporting obligations** to EU market surveillance authorities — a critical obligation for high-risk AI system providers.

**Gap:** No process for identifying, documenting, or reporting AI-related serious incidents to competent authorities under Art. 73.

---

### Summary Table of Missing Elements

| # | Article 9 Element | Present in SOP-PENG-001? | Severity |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Risk identification for health, safety, fundamental rights (Art. 9(2)(a)) | **No** — STRIDE only; no fundamental rights risk analysis | **Critical** |
| 2 | Risk evaluation under reasonably foreseeable misuse (Art. 9(2)(b)) | **Partial** — Abuser Stories exist but are security-only, not AI-misuse | **High** |
| 3 | Post-market monitoring feedback into risk management (Art. 9(2)(c) + Art. 72) | **No** — No AI performance monitoring plan; no feedback loop | **Critical** |
| 4 | Risk management hierarchy + residual risk communication (Art. 9(4)-(5)) | **Partial** — Residual risk captured but no design-first hierarchy; no deployer communication | **High** |
| 5 | AI-specific testing with pre-defined metrics/thresholds (Art. 9(5)-(8)) | **No** — Testing is purely security-focused; no model performance/robustness testing | **Critical** |
| 6 | Vulnerable groups / children impact assessment (Art. 9(9)) | **No** — No mention anywhere in the SOP | **Critical** |
| 7 | Article 13 transparency / deployer information (Art. 9(4)(c)) | **No** — No deployer-facing documentation process | **High** |
| 8 | Continuous iterative (not gate-based) risk management (Art. 9(2)) | **No** — Gate-based; no trigger-driven iterative updates | **High** |
| 9 | Documented risk management system (Art. 9(1)) | **No** — Fragmented across sections; no unified RMS | **High** |
| 10 | Serious incident reporting to authorities (Art. 73) | **No** — No AI-specific serious incident process | **High** |

---

### Bottom Line

SOP-PENG-001 is a strong **software security** SDLC, but it is fundamentally a **cybersecurity and data protection** framework. Article 9 of the EU AI Act demands an **AI risk management system** that addresses a broader set of harms — fundamental rights, health, safety, fairness, and non-discrimination — using a continuous, iterative methodology that extends well beyond traditional threat modeling and vulnerability management. To support high-risk AI systems, Meridian would need either a substantial revision of SOP-PENG-001 (adding an AI-specific risk management overlay) or a new, dedicated SOP (e.g., "SOP-AI-RMS-001") that sits alongside and integrates with the existing SSDLC, explicitly addressing all ten gaps identified above. Alignment with **ISO/IEC 23894** (AI Risk Management) and the forthcoming CEN-CENELEC harmonized standards would be the most efficient path to a presumption of conformity.

**Compliance level: SOP-PENG-001 v1.3 — NOT SUFFICIENT for EU AI Act Article 9 high-risk AI systems.**

scores.judge_rationale: The candidate answer correctly identifies all five missing elements from the reference, plus additional relevant gaps, but it does not cite specific paragraphs of Article 9 §2-§9 in a structured way, instead referencing them descriptively.
